title: Social Credit: Scores At Long Last?
text:
“C omprehensive enterprise credit assessments ” may be the most meaningful form of score or grade in China’s social credit system, but they bear little resemblance to the all-encompassing and all-defining citizen-ranking “social credit scores” that once dominated the foreign  imagination  and  media .
Businesses, not individuals, have always been the main focus of social credit. Yet the myth of citizen scoring was so pervasive that some observers even coined the term “ corporate social credit system ” to refer to the real regulatory framework, just to distinguish it from the fantasy.
Today, the mythos of the citizen scoring panopticon has been largely dispelled and lingers mainly as  a social media trope , but few people are aware of what China’s Social Credit System actually looks like. Reviewing a  new plan for improving the Comprehensive Enterprise Credit Status Assessment System  is an opportunity to help correct that.
1. BACKGROUND
Throughout its development, social credit has generally included three main pillars:
Credit Regulation (  信用监管) , also called “Credit-based Regulation”, primarily refers to the idea that government agencies should treat regulatory subjects differently based on their record of compliance with laws and regulations. This means increasing scrutiny of those with past violations, and offering facilitated services to those in good standing.
Credit Reporting   (征信),  like most familiar financial credit systems, is concerned with measuring individuals’ likelihood of defaulting on their loans and contractual obligations. To encourage credit lending, China hopes to create a well-regulated system of private  credit reporting  institutions that offer assessments of businesses’ and individuals’ financial credit.
Creditworthiness  (诚信)  is a piece of China’s ubiquitous ‘Core Socialist Values’ morality campaign, and seeks to foster honesty and integrity among citizens, organizations, and government agencies.
2. Credit Assessments
Basic Structure:
At the highest level, the plan separates two basic types of assessment:  public credit assessments and market credit assessments.
In late 2019,  public credit assessments  for businesses were advanced  as a key component of the Credit Regulation framework . The new implementation plan discusses the relationship between public and market credit assessments, and in doing so helps clarify the relationship between the Credit Regulation and Credit Reporting systems.
In the social credit context, the public/market distinction has previously been used to distinguish between the types of information used to determine a subject’s credit status:
Public Credit Information  is defined as information produced or acquired by state authorities (or authorized organizations) in the performance of their legally-prescribed duties. This includes information on any licenses or permits granted to a subject, or penalties for misconduct).
In 2021, the central government began regularly publishing official  Basic Catalogs of Public Credit Information  to be used in credit regulation.
Market Credit Information , sometimes called “non-public credit information”, is a broader category of information collected by private entities such as credit service institutions.
A. PUBLIC CREDIT ASSESSMENTS  are primarily based on public credit information. They are described as being created by government agencies to reflect a subject’s compliance with laws and regulations, and are used as a basis for regulation. In short, they are at the heart of Credit Regulation as described above.
Public Credit Assessments are themselves divided into two categories:
Industry Public Credit Assessments   – are sector-specific measurements of compliance conducted by the various regulatory authorities for use within their jurisdiction. In line with the concept of Credit Regulation, industry regulators are to treat enterprises differently based on these assessments of past compliance with the relevant laws and regulations.
Table 1: Overview of Credit Assessment Types
The central government regulatory bodies for each sector are responsible for making rules for that sector’s industry assessments. They are instructed to include indexes of information to be considered, the procedures, and potential uses of the results. If a central authority has not yet made rules, the local regulatory authorities under it are generally precluded from creating and implementing their own credit assessments without first seeking approval.
Comprehensive Public Credit     Assessments    are an attempt to measure entities’ more global legal compliance. The National Development and Reform Commission (NDRC) is responsible for developing the rules for these assessments.
Inputs and Outputs
The   inputs   for both types of Public Credit Assessment are usually limited to information from the  Public Credit Information Catalog , but can include other relevant information acquired or generated by government agencies when performing their duties.  A catalog of information for inclusion in comprehensive public credit assessments was provided with the plan.
The   output of public credit assessments   is to be a grade, usually on a 4-grade scale, of A, B, C, or D. If a more complicated point system is used, the government agency should disclose what score ranges translate to what grades. The broader rules for making assessments are also to be made public.
Assessment information is to be available to regulatory departments through the National Credit Information Sharing Platform. They may be made publicly available by the relevant government authority, either proactively or upon application. They will be available through both the Credit China website and the website of the department that made them, where they can be accessed by the subject of the report and others that they authorize.
Relationship of Comprehensive and Industry Public Credit Assessments
Comprehensive and Industry public credit assessments are intended to inform each other.
Naturally, the comprehensive assessments should reflect regulatory activity in individual sectors, and the relevant departments are asked to share information listed in the  assessment data director y   via the National Credit Information Sharing Platform.
At the same time, industry assessments are to include the comprehensive public credit assessment outcome as a factor, although the weight given to it may vary. The NDRC is to send comprehensive assessment outcomes to all relevant departments and regional governments.
One hard rule is that if an entity has a D rating in the comprehensive assessment, it cannot receive an A in any industry assessment. This means that a D in the comprehensive assessment precludes receiving preferential treatment in any sector.
B. MARKET CREDIT ASSESSMENTS  are designed to reflect a business’s reliability in market activities, and may be consulted in making lending and financing decisions.
They are usually produced by credit reporting institutions authorized (under the management of the People’s Bank of China) as commercially available products based on government data and judicial information, as well as information from industry associations, individual businesses, creditors, and business counterparts. The plan explicitly encourages the consideration of public credit assessments as a factor in market credit assessments.
Simply put, market assessments use public and market credit information to assess the likelihood of an entity defaulting on loans or contracts. In other words, market credit assessments are concerned with the  Credit Reporting  aspect of social credit.
The plan does not expressly divide market credit assessments into two categories, but does include a meaningful distinction: With the guidance of regulatory authorities, quasi-regulatory institutions such as Industry Associations and Chambers of Commerce may also carry out non-profit market credit assessments for use in industry discipline and management, so long as they do not treat members and non-members differently.
While this second type of market credit assessment is correctly categorized as a ‘market’ assessment because it is not produced by government authorities, it seems fundamentally distinct from those made by credit reporting institutions. All market credit assessments are defined by their focus on measuring the risk of default in market activities, but here that risk is to be used in industry discipline and management, a quasi-regulatory function. The required supervision of the regulatory authorities highlights the awkward fit, as those authorities’ expertise is limited to legal compliance rather than business conduct.
In context, this second type of market credit assessment is likely only meant to build on the expertise of industry associations and chambers of commerce regarding the corresponding local or sectoral business environments, allowing them to identify well-established, reliable businesses and those subject to frequent complaints. This is a potentially useful service for members and consumers that could help maintain industry standards and reputation, but it is worth highlighting as a distinct form of credit assessment.
C. Additional Applications of Credit Assessments
3. Remedies: Credit Repair and Assessment Objections
China has already been developing  a system for public credit repair,  the purging of records on non-compliance. Generally speaking, these rules require that information on violations remain publicly visible and available to be relied on in regulation for a minimum period, after which the impacted entity can apply to have it removed once they have remedied that problem. Once repaired, regulators, credit reporting institutions, etc. are all to remove the relevant information.
The Plan also includes processes for objecting to public credit assessment results. The procedures remain vague, but require a review by the department that made the assessment, with the possibility of a correction.
Objections can also be made to third-party institutions regarding either inaccurate or incomplete information provided by that entity, OR problems with the outcome of a market credit assessment they created. Again, the procedures remain vague, and there is no information on further remedies via government agencies or the courts. The rules do mention, however, that the third-party must not demand compensation for correcting or updating information.
4. Further Integration?
The new plan provides useful clarity on the direction of social credit assessments. The relationship between Public Credit Assessments and Market Credit Assessments is more clearly mapped onto the Credit Regulation and Credit Reporting framework than ever before, and there is new guidance on how the two interact, as discussed above.
The policy going forward is for public assessments to serve as a foundation for future integration with market credit assessments, to build a unified, comprehensive enterprise credit assessment. But this raises further questions:
First, to what end?  Public credit assessments are based on records of legal compliance and used in credit regulation to alert the authorities about the appropriate level of regulatory scrutiny. Market credit assessments purport to measure the risk of an entity defaulting in business activities based on their past market practice. But what is the function of the proposed integrated assessment?  Would the resulting A,B,C,D provide anything beyond what the existing assessments and data provide?
Second, what of “creditworthiness”?   Creditworthiness (诚信) is the most subjective of the three pillars of social credit, attempting to combine general morality, professional ethics, good faith dealings, and fulfilment of social responsibility. Social Credit documents addressing it have generally focused more on publicity and education rather than any form of enforcement. While some regions have nominally awarded models of creditworthiness, the law has been clear that punishments are only appropriate for violations of law, not ‘creditworthiness’ alone.
Still, more than a third of the text of  a draft Social Credit    Law  released  in 2022 focused on ‘creditworthiness’, and it seems unlikely to simply disappear. It has been suggested that indicators of creditworthiness could even be included in credit reporting’s market assessments of the risk of loan default, particularly for those who have no history of borrowing. While not much is needed in this area if creditworthiness is to play only an educational role, guidance on how such information can be used, either alone or in other assessments,  would be welcome.

title: The Friends We’ve Made 2: Changes from the Draft
text:
The discussion below builds on our discussion of the draft version of these regulations, emphasizing the changes that have been made in the final version.  A table showing and explaining those changes can be found below.
The Friends We’ve Made: An Initial Breakdown of Draft Rules for Human-like AI
Our review of China's draft measures on regulating Chatbots.
Major changes from the draft include:
Coverage narrowed to address  sustained  emotional interactions, with specific exceptions for some applications.
Requirement that all users provide emergency contacts.
Allows broader use of interaction data for model training
Shift in provisions to protect elderly users from patronizing restrictions, to empowerment
Removes the requirement for human intervention during user
China’s new  Provisional Measures on the Administration of Human-like Interactive Artificial Intelligence Services,  now jointly released by the CAC, NDRC, MIIT, and MPS, will take effect this summer, on July 15, 2026.
These Measures are an admirable attempt to get out in front of challenges that much of the world is just becoming aware of: the mental health impact of interacting regularly with non-human, virtual companions. While that sentence may read like science fiction, the issues are already pressing enough that medical professionals and policymakers are paying attention.
For better or worse, it is becoming increasingly common for people to interact with AI services that present as intelligent. That may mean asking ChatGPT for advice on navigating an awkward situation, chatting with an AI boyfriend or girlfriend, or keeping an AI service open in a browser tab as a running companion while brainstorming at work. Whatever the form, chatbots are becoming part of our social reality.
Unfortunately, these AI interactions can create risks that differ from ordinary media because AI is responsive, adaptive, and able to simulate understanding. It can build trust, shape decisions, and sustain emotionally significant interactions in ways that static content cannot, while also collecting data and personalizing influence. Those features take the risk beyond false output and into dependency, privacy intrusion, and the quiet displacement of a user’s own judgment.
Perhaps in recognition of the challenge for any legal system to keep abreast of changing technology, China has labeled these Measures as ‘provisional’, but the new rules reflect one of the most complete attempts to regulate this area.
Scope and terminology
The draft version of these regulations defined the covered technology as:
AI Products and services,
That simulate human traits such as personality, thinking, and communication,
To interact with humans emotionally,
Through text, images, audio, video, or other means.
This broad definition left questions as to what technologies were included, and the final version has made a number of key changes to narrow the scope, although it is still far from clear:
1.“Sustained”   Emotional Interaction  .  Although the rules still don’t define the emotional interaction requirement, it is now clearer that the Measures’ central concern is ongoing emotional engagement that can lead to emotional dependence and addiction.
The use of  “sustained”  to qualify the emotional interactions closely resembles  California’s SB 243 , which defines “companion chatbots” as systems able to “sustain a relationship across multiple interactions,” and may be intended in a similar sense. This would make the ability to ‘remember’ and ‘learn’ between sessions would probably be the key factor in determining coverage.
Some have suggested that the rules are meant to encompass only services specifically aimed at providing emotional services or eliciting deep emotional responses, but there isn’t much to support that. For example, as in the draft, digital relatives and companions (伴侣- a term encompassing ‘significant others’)  are mentioned as a subcategory of the covered services. More importantly, the types of harms addressed aren’t limited to such services. Self-harm, extreme emotional reactions, and obsessive dependence have been associated with even  mainstream LLMs  outside of any role-playing function.
The better understanding is the emphasis on ‘sustained relationships’ rather than trying to quantify emotional engagement.
2, Specific Excluded Uses    .    Again echoing   California’s SB 243  , the Chinese rules narrow the category of covered AI by creating express carve-outs for certain uses. They do not apply to services such as customer service, informational Q&A, work assistance, study and education, or scientific research, which do not involve sustained emotional interaction. This loosely tracks the first set of exceptions in the California bill, although that bill clarifies that the exception applies where the service is “  only  ” used for the listed purposes, helping resolve the issue of general-use chatbots, which may sometimes have these functions.
Human-like AI Provisions
CA SB 243,  b(2)(A)
1.       customer service
2.       informational Q&A
3.       work assistance
4.       study and education
5.       or scientific research
1.       customer service
2.       a business’s operational purposes
3. Productivity and analysis related to source information
4.       internal research
5.       or technical assistance.
The Chinese rules also do not incorporate SB 243’s additional exclusions for bots in video games that engage solely on game-related topics and do not involve sexually explicit conduct or mental health issues, or for standalone devices such as smart speakers that function as personal assistants.  Under China’s new rules, one would have to consider whether a specific service amounted to “sustained emotional interaction”.
3. Only   services   and not   products mentioned.    This is likely less concerned with the scope of coverage than it reflects a somewhat arcane, formalist (but not trivial) distinction in tort law. In China as in many countries, harm caused by a product can invoke special non-fault liability, while recovering from services generally requires a showing of fault. Put simply, classifying AI chatbot companions as services makes private litigation less attractive and, in some cases, more difficult. That fits China’s broader regulatory approach in this area, which emphasizes ex ante compliance obligations imposed on service providers rather than ex post accountability through individual lawsuits.
II． Protections
General Requirements:
As in the draft, the Providers of human-like AI services have the primary responsibility for the safety and security of their services.  (Art. 9) They are responsible for security throughout the entire lifecycle of the products and services, from design to implementation, and including all updates and revisions. Updates in security and functions are to be concurrently updated, so that there is no lag in protection for new features. (article 10)
The ‘security’ mentioned here is wide-ranging and touches on other laws to include: data security, pre-publication content reviews, and cybersecurity. Some categories listed in the draft as basic security issues, including personal information protection, and fraud prevention were removed in the final version from this, but are still addressed throughout the law  (Art. 9).
Special security assessments  must be conducted and reported to provincial authorities when services introduce new functions or cause major changes to existing functions, or have high user volumes (1M or more registered/ 100,000 or more monthly active users). Security assessments may also be triggered by a possible impact on national security or the public interest.  (articles 22,23).
While this section was largely unchanged from the draft, there are a few notable changes. First, provincial-level cybersecurity departments are empowered to notify service providers that they should conduct an assessment. Second, potential harms to the rights and interests of individuals or organizations, rather than to broader public interests, are no longer a trigger for assessments.
1. Prohibited Service Conduct
The basic list of prohibited service conduct has been moved to Article 8, and significantly revised since the draft.
Section 8(1) now consolidates restricted content categories, largely incorporating China’s  broader online content controls  into this new context. This includes content endangering national security, harming the national honor and interests, conducting illegal religious activities, disrupting order, and undermining ethnic unity, as well as promoting gambling, obscenity, encouraging crimes, and conduct that impacts others’ rights, such as defamation.
One interesting note is that content going against the Core Socialist Values (a ubiquitous morality and civics campaign) is prohibited. While content supporting the Core Socialist Values is generally encouraged in China’s content control regime, this feels like an increased level of protection.
Article 8(5) and (6) more clearly address mental health impacts, AI’s tendency towards of ingratiating sycophancy is specifically identified as a potential threat to users’ social relationships, replacing the narrower and more objective standard of ‘providing false information’ in the draft. The threat of inducing ‘unreasonable’ or irrational decision making remains from the draft, but the prohibition now addresses ‘emotional’ rather than ‘algorithmic’ manipulation.
A new section at article 8(4) is added to directly address special content restrictions for minors, incorporating by reference the  Measures for Categorizing Online Information that Might Impact Minors’ Physical and Psychological Health .
Prohibitions on content that encourages or glorifies self-harm, insults personal dignity, or endangers confidential information such as state secrets, work secrets, trade secrets, and personal information remain in place (Art. 8(2),(3)).
2. Mental Health Impact.
The final version of the rules remains most concerned with  excessive dependency   or addiction  to the services and  extreme emotional responses or mental health emergencies,  such as risks of suicide and self-harm (but notably not harm to others). (art.9)
To  prevent these risks , a number of clear preventative and response measures are required.  For example, Article 18 requires  conspicuous notices that the chat is with an AI and not a human being  in addition to China’s  normal AI labeling regime.   Similarly, reminders to take a break must also be displayed after every 2 hours of continuous use.
More dynamic reminders, such as pop-up window alerts, are required when users are identified as at risk of over-reliance, although this is a vaguer standard. Curiously, the draft had required an initial registration and the beginning of new sessions, but this relatively uninvasive requirement has been removed.
Interestingly, the risks involved are not limited to threats to health, and include even  risks to property . (article13). This language has been slightly qualified to include only  major  property risks to users that are currently occurring or have already happened.
Risk Identification and Response
The rules maintain the draft’s approach of having  escalating levels of intervention .
Where a “ tendency”  or ‘inclination’ towards addiction or dependency is detected, for example, pop-up windows and other more aggressive means are to be used to remind users that they are chatting with a non-human, etc. (art. 18).  When any user seeks to exit the service, it must be convenient and immediate, with no further interaction that might impede the users’ exit. (Art. 19).
Where users exhibit extreme emotional states, the service is to offer users consolation or comfort and encourage the user to seek help. (A requirement of having prepared templates was dropped from the draft). Where the situation escalates to clear expressions of intent to harm themselves, or threats of major damage, the necessary measures may be taken, such as providing assistance information or reaching out to the user’s emergency contact person (art. 13). (Emergency contact person information was previously only requested from the elderly, but this now applies to all users).
A proposal in the draft to require human intervention when users are in crises has been removed, but such an intervention could still be included in the mentioned ‘necessary measures.’
Serious questions still remain regarding  how risks are identified . The rules still do not clarify if this process should be made through human or machine review, and either approach raises serious concerns. Human review invites intrusions on privacy, while machine review requires relying on the algorithm that is the source of the problem.
Special protection for vulnerable populations
Companionship for the elderly, childcare, and meeting the needs of special groups are among the encouraged future uses for human-like AI, and the rules also introduce special protections for such groups. (Art. 6).
Some of the most drastic changes from the draft involve elderly users. The draft adopted a paternalistic approach, prohibiting their use of services that roleplayed as their family members. These prohibitions, presumably meant to protect them from fraud or confusion, have been removed.
As mentioned above, the requirement that only elderly users provide an emergency contact person has also now been expanded to all users, acknowledging that there is no reason to portray the elderly as lacking competency.
The treatment of the elderly has been reframed as emphasizing support rather than special requirements and prohibitions. Additional services and assistance are to be made available as needed. (Art. 15).
Changes to the  protections for Minors  are less sweeping, but mainly because the existing law on the protection of minors is already more established. As mentioned above, rules on content harmful to minors have now been incorporated as well, in addition to special rules on the  protection of minors’ personal information  and  general protection of minors online .
Some notable changes do address concerns raised regarding the draft, such as recognizing that minors of different ages have different needs. Article 14 now follows the  general strategy for China’s requirement of mobile internet minors modes  to focus protections on specific groups while empowering guardians to make informed individual decisions about usage time and spending etc.
A new ban on providing all minors with virtual family or companions (伴侣) has also been introduced, in accordance with a recent  draft on regulating human-appearing characters . (Art 14). The draft had only required guardian consent for companions, which is now required for minors under 14 to access any covered services.
Identification of Minors
Outside of China, identifying minors online is a contentious issue, as it requires all users, including adults, to verify their age. Increasingly, this means uploading identification documents, creating an increased risk of harmful data breaches, and making true anonymity online impossible.
China’s internet already operates on a real-name registration system, effectively requiring all users to link their national ID to most online accounts. Even this drastic measure has not proved fully effective in blocking minors’ access to improper online content; however, as kids have learned to borrow parental accounts (with and without permission), rent other adults’ accounts, and fool ID systems. ( see eg: https://finance.sina.com.cn/tech/digi/2025-12-18/doc-inhcfepi6674554.shtml )
The draft human-like AI rules seem to demand additional age verification, requiring that service providers have the capacity to identify minors. (art. 14).  This could include algorithmic detection, as was hinted at previously in the Ministry of Culture and Tourism’s    Opinions on Strengthening the Protection of Minors in the Online Cultural Market   (at 4).
Training Data and User History Information
The general data protection regime introduced in the draft was drawn from existing law, and remains in place at Article 11(1), 16, etc.)
The final paragraph of Article 15 contains a small but meaningful  change to rules on service providers’ use of interaction data . Independent consent was previously required by the draft when user interaction data OR sensitive personal information was used in model training. The final rules require independent consent only where the user information IS sensitive personal information.
Sensitive personal information is a defined category in the  Personal Information Protection Law,  referring to information that, if leaked, could easily cause injury. Under the updated language, most interaction data, including personal information that is merely identifying, may be used for training without independent consent. Instead, it must only comply with general notice and consent rules under the PIPL.
The table below indicates changes between  the draft  and finalized versions of the  Provisional Measures on the Administration of Human-like Interactive Artificial Intelligence Services
Red text indicates a deletion

title: Digital Humans vs. Human-like AI
text:
It was only a few months ago, in late December,  that China released draft  provisions on “human-like interactive AI” services  for public comment, so it was something of a surprise when a new, similar-sounding  draft on “digital virtual human” services  was released on April 3. Despite their related titles, however, the documents address very different, albeit overlapping, technologies and risks.
Scope
The earlier “ human-like AI”  draft addresses AI services that simulate human personalities, thinking, and communication to interact emotionally with human users through text, images, audio, video, or other media.
The new “ digital human ” draft is concerned with visual representations of humans, operated either by a human or a machine, that exist only in the virtual world and have characteristics such as speech, personality, OR interactive capacity.
By these definitions, a text or voice-only chatbot could be a human-like AI, but not a digital virtual human. By contrast, a  virtual news anchor ,  pop star , or  livestreaming avatar  can be a digital-human even if it isn’t automated at all, but merely controlled by a human puppeteer via motion capture sensors and filter overlays. Virtual digital humans also do not need to be interactive, and might just appear in online content.
The definitions are not mutually exclusive. Article 18 of the new digital virtual humans draft acknowledges situations where digital virtual humans (likenesses) are used to provide human-like AI services (interactive machine relationships); ie, an interactive AI that includes a visual human avatar
In sum, the  human-like AI  draft is about machines (AI) that   act like humans  , and the psychological impact of users’ relationships with them. The  digital human  draft is about regulating visual portrayals that   look like humans   as a new medium for conveying information online.
人工智能拟人化互动服务管理暂行办法
Provisional Measures on the Administration of Human-like Interactive Artificial Intelligence Services:
The finalized rules for Human-like AI were released shortly after this article was published, and are available here.
Regulatory focus:
The earlier human-like AI draft is an ambitious first effort to construct an overarching framework for a new type of human-machine relationship that blurs the line between tool and companion, but also brings novel risks to users. It focuses on service providers’ liability for the AI’s design and activity, emphasizing  requirements to protect user mental-health and prevent excessive dependence.
The digital-human draft is more typical of other Chinese regulations addressing new means of sharing information online. China often releases technology-specific regulations (such as on  instant messaging ,  livestreaming ,  online fiction , and  even user comment sections)  that incorporate and apply broader rules to the specific new context. They can also assign regulatory responsibilities among the relevant state agencies and introduce new enforcement mechanisms such as administrative penalties.
The digital-human draft thus focuses on digital virtual humans primarily as digital works and means of conveying information, rather than the unique challenges of their ‘humanity’. It incorporates rules for protecting personal information, personality rights (no clones of people without their consent), intellectual property, and data security in the creation of use of such avatars. It adapts content moderation requirements and other regulatory obligations that apply to broader media, such as algorithm filing requirements. Where violations are not covered by other laws, Article 24, authorizes a number of penalties including fines of up to 200,000 RMB, and shutting down the service.
Rather than go through each obligation, I have loosely annotated a translation below, linking to some prior sources of the protections and innovations.
First, however, it is worth considering a few of the more unique provisions:
Government digital persons:
Article 19 addresses the use of digital virtual persons to provide government services or participate in public management or judicial activities. Digital avatar  civil servants  and even  judges  have already been put into service, although their functions are usually limited to providing public information rather than decision-making.
The new draft requires that
Citizens can opt out of the digital virtual human service.
There are mechanisms for manual oversight, and
their use should be guided by the principles of necessity and reasonableness (as well as legality).
These requirements feel like a chastening reminder that not every situation needs automation, and that automation is a supplement, rather than a substitute, for government responsiveness to the public.
Identity Concealment
Article 11(5) addresses the use of digital virtual persons to evade legally required identity verification systems using tech such as facial or voice recognition. Providing and verifying real-name identity information is a requirement for receiving many online services in China, but this provision adds a more specific focus on the unique use of digital humans to evade biometrics.
This article, and Article 11(7)’s recitation of existing prohibitions on improperly registering and trading accounts, are also reminiscent of the recent draft  Cybercrime Law ’s efforts to reinforce the real-name system.
Protections for Human Operators
Article 11(6) contains a brief acknowledgment of the rights of the human drivers of some digital humans. It bars infringement of the drivers’ rights and interests, such as in their personal information or the right to freely choose employment.
This brief provision may conceal some big issues. As with traditional performers, the real person operating a digital human may become inextricably associated with the character they perform. And in an employment relationship, the blurred identity of actor and character, operator and avatar, can create difficult questions.
For example, when does actors’ subsequent work infringe the employer’s rights in a proprietary character? Is Harrison Ford infringing on the Indiana Jones IP if he wears a fedora (or carries a whip, etc.) to film an unrelated cola ad? Are there any limits on what the employer can portray the character, with Mr. Ford’s likeness, doing?
These issues aren’t new to digital humans, but could become more prominent. A digital human may be created by capturing a performer’s face, voice, motions, or other biometric information, making it even more visually associated with the character. At some point, disputes over control of a digital human may become disputes over the performer’s personal information, reputation, and freedom to choose future work.
Protection of Minors.
Many of the requirements of Article 10 incorporate existing protections for minors online:
No inducements to spending ( Law on the Protection of Minors 74)
No inducements to religion ( Internet Religious Information Services regs art. 14 )
Prohibit content that might impact minors’ physi cal  or mental health. ( Measures for Categorizing Online Information that Might Impact Minors’ Physical and Psychological Health )
Prevent Addiction ( Regulations on the Protection of Minors Online, 42 )
More interesting, however, are  requirements that minors must not be provided with emotionally resonant digital virtual human services, such as virtual family or companions . This seems to address the emotional dependence risks that are more typical of the  Human-like AI provisions  than this draft regulation. The draft of those provisions, however, stopped short of banning the provision of AI-driven companions to minors, only requiring additional guardian controls. (Article 12) The closest proposal is a prohibition of AI-simulated relations for elderly users, not minors (Article 13), in a paternalistic effort to prevent fraud.
One way to make sense of this distinction is that the concern here might not be emotional attachment at all, but a different danger unique to human-operated digital persons: adults using virtual avatars as disguises to befriend or groom minors.
UPDATE: Shortly after the publication of this article, the Human-like AI rules were finalized with important changes to the relevant content. The prohibition on virtual relatives and partners for minors is now a part of that regulation as well, and similar prohibition for elders has been removed.
Labeling Requirements:
Article 13 creates an obligation to prominently label digital virtual human services with the text “digital human” written in the area where the avatar is displayed for the entire duration of the service. This is a new labeling requirement, beyond the more detailed  labeling requirements for AI-generated content , which still apply separately.
Even though virtual digital human services are not necessarily AI-generated or controlled, most will fall within the scope of China’s  deep synthesis regulations , that covers tools for generating or editing faces and voices, and also include labeling requirements. (articles 16,17). The AI labeling rules require both ‘explicit’ labels, which can be perceived by humans, and “implicit” metadata labeling. As in the AI-labeling rules, the service providers, users, and information publication platforms all bear some responsibility for this new labeling requirement.
6, Additional annotations
Chapter II: Protection of Rights and Interests
NOTES
Article 7:  Where any organization or individual uses natural persons’ sensitive personal information for modeling, image generation, scene construction, or other activities, they shall obey the laws and administrative regulations and meet the following requirements:
Personal information protection requirements
(1) Obtain the natural persons’ independent consent, and truthfully, accurately, and completely notify them, in a conspicuous fashion using clear and easily understood language, of the purposes of the handling, its necessity, and its impact on personal rights and interests, as well as other matters provided for by laws and administrative regulations; and where the personal information of minors less than14 years-old is used, the independent consent of their parents or other guardians shall be obtained. Except as otherwise specified by laws and administrative regulations.
7(1)  recites the requirements for obtaining independent informed consent to handle sensitive personal information, including giving conspicuous and understandable notice of the purpose, necessity, and impact on rights from handling the information. ( PIPL  17, 28-31)
(2) After a natural person withdraws their consent, employ methods such as deletion of relevant personal information to eliminate the impact, and personal information must not be retained in any form or used for other purposes, except as otherwise provided by laws and administrative regulations. Except where parties have otherwise agreed, the digital virtual human shall also be cancelled.
7(2) requires digital virtual human service providers to delete sensitive personal information after consent is revoked in accordance with  PIPL  article 47 . It goes slightly further, however, by saying that the digital virtual human itself may be cancelled, unless the parties otherwise agreed.
(3) Respect the lawful rights and interests of others; the rights and interests of others in their image, reputation, honor, privacy, and personal information must not be infringed. Where the personal information of the deceased is used to carry out related activities, the deceased’s relatives may lawfully exercise corresponding rights in the deceased’s personal information for their own lawful and legitimate interests, except where the deceased made other arrangements before their death.
7(3) addresses the rights of family members in deceased relative’s personal information in accordance with PIPL article 49, which allows them to seek access, reproduction, correction, or deletion of the information, unless the deceased made agreements to the contrary before death.
Curiously 7(3) goes beyond the PIPL to address surviving family members’ rights to exercise broader personality rights beyond personal information rights, a concept now found in  Civil Code Article  994.
Article 8:  Any organization and individual providing or using digital virtual human services must not malign, deface, or infringe on the personality rights of others in any form, and digital virtual human services that can identify the identity of a specified natural person must not be provided without the consent of that specified natural person. Including but not limited to:
Article 8 is largely an adaptation of existing civil code provisions preventing unauthorized and harmful uses of individuals names and appearances. ( Civil Code  1017, 1019)
One potentially new wrinkle is that the article incorporates and idea similar to the definition of personal information- information from which a natural persons’ identity may be determined. It says that where a digital virtual human service is similar enough in name or likeness to a real human that it identifies that person, their consent must be acquired before the service is provided to anyone. This language isn’t typical of personality rights discussions,  but past rules on deepfakes  have required consent before manipulating persons faces and voices. ( Deep Synthesis Regulations Art. 14 )
(1) Using the pen name, stage name, online alias, translated name, brand, name, or shortened name of other persons that have a definite degree of social recognizability;
(2) Using likeness or voice that is highly similar to specified natural persons.
Article 9:  Any organizations or individuals providing or using digital virtual human services shall respect intellectual property rights and commercial ethics. The intellectual property rights lawfully enjoyed by others must not be infringed upon in using others’ text, art, photography, music, audio-video, or other works or pieces to produce digital virtual humans, as well as during the use of digital virtual humans by any type of entity.
Article 9 simply states that intellectual property rights must be respected in the creation and use of digital virtual humans. This follows earlier more general provisions on generative AI products, but provides little guidance on what constitutes an IP violation, ie: What is the scope of fair use? ( Generative AI Regs , article 4(3), 7(2)).
Article 10:  It is prohibited to entice minors to become addicted to digital virtual human services. Minors must not be provided with virtual relatives, virtual companions, and other virtual close relations, digital virtual human services that induce excessive spending or religious belief, as well as digital virtual human services that contain information that might impact the physical and psychological health of minors such that which might lead or induce minors to imitate unsafe behavior, carry out acts that violations social mores, produce extreme emotions, or cultivate bad habits.
Minors
No virtual close relations
No inducements to spending ( Law on the Protection of Minors 74)
No inducements to religion ( Internet Religious Information Services regs art. 14 )
Might impact minors physical or mental health
Chapter III: Regulation of Services
Article 11:  Any organization or individual providing or using digital virtual human services shall obey the laws and administrative regulations, respect social mores, ethics, and morality, and must not engage in any of the following activities:
Providers and users prohibited conduct:
(1) Generating or disseminating content that endangers the nation’s security, honor, and interests; incites subversion of state sovereignty or the overturning of the socialist system; incites separatism or undermines national unity; advocates terrorism, extremism, or historical nihilism; goes against the Core Socialist Values, public order, and good custom; carries out illegal religious activities; promotes ethnic hatred and discrimination; stirs up opposition between groups, disseminates obscenity, pornography, gambling, violence, or the instigation of crimes; spreads rumors; or insults or defames others, harming the lawful rights and interests of other.;
Illegal content categories;  Regulation of Online Information Content E-cosystem art. 6)
(2) Including content that harms the nation’s image in the design, dress or symbols, personality or preferences of digital virtual humans;
2) character design that harms nation’s image.
Similar to rules for online account names.
Also similar to removed
(3) Distorting or maligning the images of persons such as heroes and martyrs, twisting or altering the deeds and spirit of heroes and martyrs, using the images of persons such as heroes and martyrs for commercial purposes;
Heroes martyrs, leaders
Law on Protection of Heroes and Martyrs  22
(4) Engaging in illegal activities such as false advertising for goods or services, maliciously inducing purchases, or telecommunications fraud;
Fraud and advertising violations
Measures on the Administration of Internet Advertising
Law on Countering Telecommunications Network Fraud
(5) Using virtual digital humans to evade facial recognition, voice recognition, or other identity authentication mechanisms when laws or administrative regulations require that real identity information is to be provided;
Evading online protection systems
(6) Infringing on the personal information rights, right to choose one’s profession, or other lawful rights and interests of the real-person driver of a human-driven virtual human;
Protection for human driver.
(7) Improperly registering or trading internet accounts;
Evasion  2
(8) other conduct in violation of laws and administrative regulations.
Article 12:  Any organization or individual providing or using digital virtual human services shall employ measures to consciously defend against and reject the generation and transmission of content with a negative impact on the online ecology, such as sexual innuendo or suggestive flirtation; horror or cruelty, or incitement of group discrimination.
Negative information prevention
Regulation of Online Information Content E-cosystem art. 7)
Article 13:   From the start of digital virtual human services, the digital virtual human service providers, service users, and the providers of online information content transmission services shall continuously display a conspicuous alert containing the text “digital human” in the area where the digital virtual human is presented for the entire process, and are to comply with state provisions related to the labeling of AI generated or synthesized content.
Labeling:
Continuous throughout service
Compare:  Measures for  Labeling  of  AI -Generated Synthetic Content
Article 14:  Digital virtual human service providers and service users shall follow laws and administrative regulations to carry out data handling activities for specified purposes and within a specified scope, use data from lawful sources and implement responsibility for data security protections, employ suitable technical measures and other necessary measures to ensure the security of data storage and transmission, to prevent the leakage or improper use of data.
Providers and users : DATA Handling
–          follow laws
–          lawful sources
–          protections
Data Security Law , eg: articles 27, 32
Article 15:  Digital virtual human service providers and service users shall establish mechanisms such as for monitoring digital virtual human services’ security risks, early warning and emergency response, and prevention of addiction, and establish and improve systems for managing content orientation; allot technical capacities and personnel corresponding to the scale of operations, employ methods that combine technical measures such as AI and big data with manual review; strengthen the identification, monitoring, and early warnings for digital virtual human services, and record and store log information.
Providers and users: Risk Management
Must have appropriate means and staff to review,
Where it is discovered that digital virtual human services are being used to engage in unlawful activities, measures such as dynamic identity verification, warnings, limited functions, or terminating services shall be promptly employed; and where major risks are discovered, the digital virtual human services shall be immediately suspended or terminated, deregistering the digital persons and eliminating the impact.
Violations using  DVH
Consequences of suspending services.
Article 16:  Digital virtual human service providers shall sign service agreements with technical support providers and service users, clarifying and ensuring content safety as well as content on the rights and obligations, such as data collection, use, and storage.
User and tech agreements on safety obligations
Article 17:  Service providers that provide services for the transmission of online information content shall establish and improve systems for managing the orientation of content, allocate technical capacities and personnel corresponding to the scope of operations, perform content safety management responsibilities, optimize content review and account management responsibilities, strengthen management of user-published information, promptly address illegal and negative information, and store log information.
Content transmission- must have content orientation management , necessary staff etc.
Article 18:  Where digital virtual human services are used to provide human-like interactive artificial intelligence services, and users select to cancel specified service functions or leave the service, they must not be tricked or excessively induced to continue the services.
Alignment with human-like provisions
-no obstacles to exit
Encourage the adoption of necessary measures to conduct active intervention and professional assistance for user inclinations towards suicide, self-harm, or other threats to health and life.
Human-like dependence protections
Article 19:  When digital virtual human services are used in areas such as government services, public management, and judicial activities, the principles of legality, reasonableness, propriety, and necessity shall be complied with, mechanisms for manual oversight review shall be set up; and users shall have the right to choose whether to accept or reject the digital virtual human services.
In official settings, users can reject DVH
Chapter IV: Oversight Inspections and Legal Responsibility
Article 20:  Digital virtual human service providers and the providers of services for the transmission of online information content shall establish mechanisms for digital virtual human users’ appeals and public complaints and reports, set up convenient portals for appeals, complaints, and reports, and promptly address them and give feedback on the outcome.
Providers-
Report and complaint portals
Article 21:  Digital virtual human service providers that have public opinion properties or the capacity for social mobilization shall perform formalities for filing algorithms and the modification or cancellation of filings in accordance with the  Provisions on the Management of Algorithmic Recommendations in Internet Information Services.
Public opinion properties, File algorithms
Digital virtual human technical support providers are to perform filings, modifications, and cancellations of filings with reference to the preceding paragraph.
Tech support- also filings
Article 22:  Digital virtual human service providers providing internet information service with a public opinion character or having the ability to mobilize the public shall carry out security assessments in accordance with relevant state provisions.
Security assessments for public mobilization
Article 23:  Cybersecurity and informatization departments and relevant departments are to carry out oversight inspections of digital virtual human services on the basis of their duties. Digital virtual human service providers, technical support providers, service users, and the providers of services that provide services for the transmission of online information content shall cooperate in accordance with law and provide the necessary support and assistance.
Oversight inspections by CAC.
Cooperation by involved parties.
Article 24:  Where these provisions are violated and laws and administrative regulations have other provisions, punishment is to be given in accordance with those provisions and civil liability is to be borne in accordance with law; where laws and administrative organs are silent, the relevant departments are to give warnings, circulate criticism, or order that corrections be made in a set period of time on the basis of their duties; and where corrections are refused, or the circumstances are serious, they are to order that the provision of services stops, and may give a concurrent fine of between 10,000 and 100,000 RMB; and where it involves harmful consequences endangering citizens’ life or health, a concurrent fine of between 10,000 and 200,000 is to be given.
General Punishment authority in the absence of other law.

title: China’s Draft Cybercrime Law
text:
Updated with additional content and  cleaning up typos 2/19/2026,
Key Points:
Reinforces the real-name registration system, further decreasing anonymity online
Further codifies the Great Firewall and threatens VPNs
Extraterritorial Application, codification of entry bans for those spreading false information about China abroad.
New administrative penalties for assisting illegal conduct online
Heightened protections for cyberviolence victims
First-ever penalties for possession of CSAM (child sexual abuse material)
As more of our lives migrate online, crime has been no exception, and China has developed a growing body of  legal authority  aimed at the prevention,  investigation , and trial of online crimes. This includes both new procedures, such as for the collection and treatment of  electronic evidence , and approaches to new forms of crime, such as  telecom fraud ,  cyberviolence ,  and the online sexual abuse of minors . A new draft  Cybercrime Law  (formally the “Law on the Prevention and Control of Cybercrime”) released by the Ministry of Public Security aggregates and builds upon some of this authority.
Despite its name, the draft law does not create new criminal offenses (which must be found in the criminal code) but emphasizes prevention by seeking to disrupt the tools, services, and conditions that enable online crime. It is an incredibly packed law, laying out the obligations of various government and private sector actors in combating cybercrime, and authorizing administrative penalites for enabling cybercrime.
The draft is already controversial, with some commentators concerned that it increases online surveillance, fortifies censorship, and extends long-arm jurisdiction to conduct overseas. While it is not possible to quickly summarize the entire draft, this article examines these concerns and provides an overview of key new provisions.
Online Surveillance: Strengthening the Real Name Registration System
Chapter II of the Draft Cybercrimes Law, titled “ Management of Basic Network Resources,”  primarily works to strengthen China’s online real-name registration system by closing the paths used to circumvent it.
The existing real-name registration system requires online service providers and platforms to collect users’ “real identity information” before providing them with basic network resources like network access, payment systems, content publishing, or instant messaging. In most contexts online, users are free to use a front-facing alias to maintain anonymity towards other users, but must register their true identity behind the scenes.
The best way to understand the real-name registration system is to view it as the online equivalent of the ubiquitous CCTV cameras that monitor China’s physical public spaces. Not all online conduct is continuously monitored in real time, but the system creates a record allowing online activity to be traced back to identifiable users.  As with security cameras, the system is viewed as essential to both solving and deterring crime.
Service providers usually verify users’ identity information through direct uploads of citizen ID cards or by linking accounts to users’ phone numbers, which are themselves linked to ID numbers. The government now also offers an  online authentication service  that allows users to verify their identities through a central public system to avoid providing as much personal information directly to platforms.
Articles 11-13  of the new draft shift away from the real-name system’s usual emphasis on providers’ obligations to verify IDs, to instead focus on prohibited user conduct that evades or undermines the system. This includes using fake IDs to open accounts or sharing network and phone connections, payment methods, information-sharing accounts, and web hosting. Foreign telecom and finance tools are specifically mentioned because they do not generally comply with China’s real-name registration requirements and facilitate transferring funds abroad.
Table1: Prohibited user conduct undermining the real-name registration system
Carrying out any of the above conduct is not a ‘crime’ under the draft, but an administrative violation, which can be directly punished by the police or appropriate regulatory agencies. Violations of any of these three articles are punishable by a fine of between 1 and 10 times the income earned from the misconduct, or up to 200,000 RMB, and even 15 days detention where the circumstances are serious.
Administrative agencies are also authorized to blacklist offending individuals or organizations and order service providers to restrict blacklisted users’ access to services. This may sound trivial and reasonably proportionate, but in China’s mobile-driven culture and economy, losing internet and phone access, or even just access to key payment apps, can make it difficult to perform basic daily tasks, approaching a kind of digital exile. Clear procedures should be established for the closing of accounts in recognition that they are a critical asset that may contain users’ intellectual property and personal information.
Articles 16 and 17  return the focus to the service providers, clarifying that user identity verification is not a one-off task at registration, but an ongoing and dynamic process  (a point previously established in Article 15 of the  Provisions on the Management of Internet User Account Information ). Additional verification is required where there is irregular account activity, and government agencies may request either new verification of specific accounts or a general increase in reverification frequency for areas or times of high criminal activity. Users who fail verification may face restricted or terminated functionality, but they are entitled to automatic review, with services restored once verification is successfully completed.
Article 15  introduces a new dimension of real name registration targeting controlled software and services that provincial police designate as readily used in cybercrimes. The providers of this equipment must file users’ or purchasers’ real identity information with the police and regulatory authorities (such as for telecommunications).
The article’s examples of “controlled” software largely involve tools that can undermine traceability and, by extension, online surveillance. They include functions such as spoofing or masking a user’s location (for example, VPN-like services), enabling remote control of other systems, and facilitating the bulk management of multiple internet connections, devices, or accounts.
Article 14  goes further by outright banning equipment primarily designed to evade regulatory controls or facilitate cybercrime. This includes devices for bulk control of SIM cards; intercepting others’ text messages; spoofing caller ID (disguising outgoing numbers); disabling or disrupting others’ phones; and systems that mass-process SMS or voice verification codes.
Censorship: Banning VPNs?
Some of the restrictions mentioned above touch on the use of VPNs and proxies, which are of particular concern because they are used by many to circumvent content restrictions and access foreign sites.
Losing access to effective VPNs and being cut off from the global web is a regular concern for many based in China. It is not entirely unfounded, as  longstanding  rules prohibit the unauthorized establishment of international connections  or circumvention  tools, and there have been periodic  crackdowns  reported. Enforcement tends to target those  creating ,  selling , or providing others with circumvention tools, but there are confirmed incidents of people being fined for simply  using  such tools.
In the context of this draft, however, it is far from clear that there is an intention to move VPN usage further out of the gray area and towards a full prohibition, but it is worth following closely.
Draft Article 12(3), for example, prohibits using “tools for changing network addresses” to avoid real-name registration requirements and register a large number of accounts . There’s little question that VPNs are “tools for changing network addresses”, but the prohibition here is very limited: the tools must be (1) used to circumvent registration rules, (2) to register large numbers of accounts, (3) for publication and instant messaging services.  This seems less focused on individuals ‘scaling the firewall’ to access foreign content, and more clearly aimed at preventing mass sending of fraudulent or phishing information, or registering multiple accounts to launch an attack like that recently aimed at  the Kuaishou streaming platform .
Article 15(2) requires that the creators or providers of tools for ‘virtual positioning’   (虚拟定位)  file users’ or purchasers’ real identity information with the police and other authorities.  It is less obvious that ‘virtual positioning’ refers to VPNs, but it certainly could include them. The thrust of the full Article 15, however, is on equipment that could be “widely used for cybercrimes or violations,” with the other two listed examples being tools for bulk control of accounts, network lines, or smart devices, and tools for accessing and controlling computer systems. As with Article 12 above, and the entirety of Chapter II, this seems mainly aimed at preventing fraud and hacking.
Article 44, however, is much more directly on point as it specifically prohibits the production, sale, or provision of means to assist or support in the access of blocked illegal foreign content . It is worth noting, however, that it is aimed only at the providers of these tools, not users, which is akin to current practice. Article 58 contains the penalties for the violation, making clear that it remains an administrative violation rather than a crime, with possible penalties of up to 10x illegal gains or up to 50,000 RMB if there are none, and even up to 15 days detention where circumstances are serious.
Finally, VPNs are also directly mentioned in article 39(2) where detecting illegally established VPNs (alongside viruses, botnets, and denial of service attacks) is listed among the obligations of hosting and cloud storage services. It is interesting that the detection of VPNs is only outsourced to this type of service, and unclear how rigorously they can and will perform it.
Great Fire Wall and Illegal Content
While China’s internet controls, including blocking sites like Twitter and Facebook, are well known, their legal basis was not always clear. 2016’s Cybersecurity Law (article 50) was the first time a national law directly authorized the use of technical measures to block prohibited foreign content. The draft Cybercrime Law’s Article 44 repeats this language, clarifying that the Cybersecurity Administration is in charge of coordinating other departments and network operators in this area. Article 53 calls for the technical blocking of internet services and products that support or assist online crimes.
Other provisions throughout the draft concern the  obligations  of various service providers to address illegal information online domestically. “Illegal information” is a term of art in  China’s content regulation regime , and includes politically sensitive content as well as obscenity, gambling, orinstigating crime.
Extraterritorial Reach
Disrupting the free flow of information across national borders is not solely about content controls, but also about the protection of personal information and crime prevention. The draft’s provisions on controlling cross-border criminal activity, however, have been viewed by some as efforts to extend China’s jurisdiction and control across its borders.
Article 2  of the draft makes clear that the law will apply to the prevention and control of cybercrime  within mainland China , but then also asserts two forms of additional jurisdiction:
1. “Active personality jurisdiction” – meaning that Chinese citizens can be held liable for violating the law anywhere in the world.
2. “Objective territorial jurisdiction,”  which refers to taking jurisdiction over misconduct initiated outside a nation’s borders that has a substantial impact within the country. Here, the draft says authorities will pursue the liability of foreign individuals and organizations that violate the law when providing services within China.
Neither of these appears to be an aggressive attempt to regulate abroad as violations of the law all relate to conduct with a distinct domestic impact (  see table 2  ). One could argue that China is trying to set international standards, because internet service providers seeking (direct or incidental) access to China’s market need to comply with its laws, but that isn’t unique to China or this draft law. The focus of this law is on aggressively minimizing the conditions for online misconduct in China.
In  Chapter V: The Prevention and Control of Cross-border Cybercrimes,  there are several provisions specifically aimed at foreign actors that have been flagged as asserting overseas enforcement powers.
Seizure of Assets:  Article 54 provides that where a foreign individual or entity is accused of committing crimes in China via the internet (such as fraud or gambling), the resulting criminal proceeds, including even assets purchased or invested with those proceeds, may be sealed, seized, or frozen, and ultimately confiscated following a court judgment. By its plain language, this provision does not expand the general asset-preservation and confiscation framework in the  Criminal Procedure Law ; indeed, it expressly requires that the measures be taken “in accordance with law.”
What may be newer is the article’s second sentence, which authorizes agencies to restrict the convicted party’s domestic investment. Because investment restrictions in China are typically sector-specific and tied to licensing or approval regimes, it is unclear whether this language is meant simply to encourage regulators to use existing gatekeeping powers or to establish an independent legal basis for imposing investment bans.
Unlike Article 54, no criminal conviction is required for Article 55, which authorizes sanctions, including entry bans and asset freezing, against foreign organizations and individuals that create or spread adverse “fake” information online impacting China’s national sovereignty, security, and interests. In the past, most announced sanctions against individuals and organizations for this type of “ maliciously spreading lies and disinformation”  have been framed as countersanctions, rather than direct disciplinary action. Still, in many ways, this article can be viewed as formally codifying the longstanding practice  of denying access to critics of China  under the broad authority of the nation’s immigration law.
New Enforcement
Chapter III, titled “Governance of the Cybercrime Ecosystem”,  provides for an array of administrative offenses in Articles 19-33, enforceable by both the competent regulatory agencies and the police.
Most of these are for “ knowingly ” supporting or assisting those acting illegally online, either by enabling the misconduct or concealing and converting its proceeds, such as via conversion into cryptocurrency or cash. Past interpretations concerning online crimes have indicated that the required “ knowledge ” of the other party’s misconduct can be shown through circumstances such as having been warned by authorities, charging significantly inflated rates, or even providing funding via advertising on a site with suspiciously high traffic. These provisions extend liability beyond the primary offender in cybercrime, and put a further burden on online service providers to check their clients’ legitimacy.
The remaining offenses primarily reinforce existing laws in related areas, such as advertising, personal information protection, unfair competition, etc. All of the new violations in articles 19-33, however, are enforced by the same penalty in Article 59, with fines as a multiple of the criminal proceeds or up to 50,000 RMB, and administrative detention of up to 15 days possible in serious circumstances.
Table 2: Additional New Offenses
Knowing Support  Other Offenses
Requesting Support
The draft law primarily functions by creating new crime prevention obligations for online service providers, but a few notable provisions also empower law enforcement to request support.
Article 39, for example, allows that public security organs may request services be suspended for any accounts, access cards, or network and telephone lines that are suspected of being used illegally. As mentioned above, the loss of account access in today’s society is itself a serious consequence that should not be imposed lightly as an investigative measure without serious procedural and durational limits.   A showing of necessity should be required before this power is authorized, especially as it involves only ‘suspected’ offenses, including minor violations that do not reach the level of a crime.
Article 51 is likely to attract even more foreign attention,  as it requires network services to provide technical interfaces and decryption to law enforcement in the investigation of all crimes. This builds on existing requirements in the  Cybersecurity Law , where article 30 requires that network service providers give police and state security authorities ‘technical support’ to ongoing criminal investigations and efforts to protect national security, and the  Counter-Terrorism Law , which specifies that the technical support can include providing technical interfaces and decryption when these authorities are working to prevent or investigate terrorism.
While it was likely already true in practice, the draft law explicitly removes the distinction between terror and other criminal investigations to expressly require that decryption and technical interfaces may be provided. In its current form, the draft calls for the Ministry of Public Security to take the lead in drafting specific requirements for what cooperation is entailed.
Protection of Minors
Article 50 specifically addresses offenses against minors. This has been a particular focus in China following the addition of a new online protection chapter in the 2020 revisions of the  Law on the Protection of Minors  and subsequent  Regulations on the Protection of Minors Online  (2024). The offenses covered include cyberbullying, leading them to crime, and encroaching on their privacy by releasing sealed records.
Notably, the article also includes the production, publication, and even possession of pornographic content involving minors. Possession of “CSAM” (Child Sexual Abuse Material) was made illegal for the first time in the 2020 revision of the Law on the Protection of Minors, but no penalty was provided, even in recent revisions to the Public Security Punishments Law. While the draft falls short of treating all CSAM offenses as criminal conduct with potentially lengthy jail time, it marks the first time a penalty has been explicitly authorized for possession of CSAM.
Further Protection against Cyberviolence (articles 48/49)
Complementing past provisions that allowed  courts to issue injunctions  to stop cyberviolence, the new draft  law allows the police or cybersecurity administration to directly issue ‘online protection orders.’ These orders can require network operators to promptly block offending, bullying content.
In another move, the draft brings the ‘ written warning’ system  (告诫书）introduced by the  Domestic Violence Law  into the context of cyberviolence. Written warnings are intended to allow police to both have an immediate deterrent effect and to create a basis for future action if the bad conduct continues.
Prevention Obligations of Various Service Providers
Chapter IV comprehensively lists the cybercrime security duties that must be performed by various network service providers. The ‘cybercrime’ duties generally involve both the prevention of crimes and less serious violations, encouraging more aggressive enforcement. Many of this Chapter’s articles largely recite obligations found in prior regulations and laws, but are consolidated for harmony and convenience. A brief summary can be found below:

Network Operators    Obligations to prevent their services from cybercrime OR their use in cybercrime:
Dedicated cybercrime prevention bodies or personnel
Management systems with tech measures and training
Contingency and response plans

Address leads, store records, and report to police
(35)
Network Operators  Conduct cybercrime risk assessment at key stages such as when new tech or applications are implemented (42)
