title: Suitable for All Ages? [Part 2]
Alien Chat: China’s 1st Case of Criminal Responsibility for AI-Generated Content
text:
As the rest of the world struggles with how to best address the flood of degrading images generated by Elon Musk’s Grok AI, China sentenced two operators of a Chinese “AI companion” service to prison for producing obscene materials after their chatbot engaged in sexually explicit conversations with users.
In a case that could have major consequences for China’s AI industry, the developers of Alien Chat are now asking a Shanghai court to overturn their convictions. Beyond the salacious nature of the sexualized chats involved here, the case raises important questions about legal responsibility for all AI-generated content.
This article quickly examines China’s existing laws on obscenity crimes and its newer AI regulations to tease out what is genuinely new in the case. In particular, it focuses on the issues that have been highlighted in Chinese reporting and commentary:
Authorship and Responsibility : Who made and spread the obscene content: The users entering prompts, the AI model that generated it, or the developers and service providers who made the model available?
Public Harm:  Where is the social harm in private, sexual chats between adult users and a machine, which can justify legal intervention?
Regulation and Criminal Liability:  How do regulations on AI services relate to the Criminal Law’s obscenity offenses?
Existing Law
OBSCENITY
Obscenity is just  one type of prohibited content  in China’s well-known censorship regime.
Under the Criminal Law, it is a crime to produce, reproduce, publish, sell, or disseminate obscene materials for profit. (article 363). Even without a profit motive,  dissemination  can still be illegal if the quantity of materials or the size of the audience meets specified numeric thresholds. (article 364).
“Obscene materials” are defined as including prurient (lust-inciting, lewd) materials that either (1) explicitly depict sexual activity or (2) blatantly promote pornography. An exception is left for works with scientific, medical, literary, and artistic value. On its face, this definition resembles the laws of many other countries, including the US, but is understood in practice to include most pornography.
Originally, the Criminal Law focused on traditional media (books, periodicals, film, audio-video, and images) but subsequent guidance in  2004  and  2010  has clarified how the crimes apply to online conduct involving obscene electronic information.
In determining whether a crime has been committed and evaluating its seriousness, the law considers the actual harmful consequences, illicit gains, the volume of content involved, and the reach or scale of dissemination, with specific quantitative thresholds. Non-criminal administrative public order penalties may be imposed when the thresholds for establishing a crime aren’t met.
AI REGULATION
China now has three major overlapping regulations directed at AI-related services: one governing  algorithmic recommendation , one governing  deep synthesis , and one governing  generative AI . All three impose duties on providers to address illegal content, including obscenity.
The    Algorithmic Recommendation Provisions   largely incorporate China’s  broader content-governance regime  into the new tech context: providers must stop the transmission of illegal content, preserve relevant records, and report it to the relevant authorities. (article 9)
The   Deep-Synthesis   Provisions   go further by requiring providers to strengthen monitoring and reviews of both user inputs and generated outputs for illegal content, including obscene materials. (article 10)
The   Generative AI   Measures   explicitly prohibit the generation of illegal content and emphasize provider obligations to prevent and address such outputs. (article 4)
Even more importantly for the Alien Chat case, the  G  enerative AI   M  easures also state   that provider  s   shall bear responsibilit  y   as   online   information content   producers  in accordance with law. (article 9). That might sound like it answers one of the core questions: who is the producer of generated content, but unfortunately, it’s not quite that easy.
The Generative AI Measures are administrative rules that set oversight and compliance obligations, as well as enforcement responsibility, but they can’t, on their own, interpret the scope of a criminal offense. While the Measures were jointly issued by a formidable lineup of concerned agencies [1] , the institutions that ultimately define and apply criminal liability—the legislature, courts, and procuratorate weren’t among them.
Criminal Liability for knowingly assisting obscenity crimes.
In related contexts,  China’s courts and procuratorate have previously considered the criminal liability of those who play less direct roles in the creation and dissemination of obscene materials.
For example, a  2004 Interpretation  on online obscenity crimes provides that liability as a joint offender extends to those who knowingly support others who are criminally producing, reproducing, or disseminating obscene electronic information. This support can include providing technical services such as internet access, web hosting, fee settlements, or data storage. (article 7). A  2010 follow-up interpretation  added that even those knowingly advertising on obscene websites could be considered joint offenders for providing funding. (article 7).
That interpretation further provides that the owners or managers of websites could be found guilty of disseminating obscene material for profit if they knowingly allow others to publish such content on their sites in sufficient volumes. (article 4). Similarly, telecommunication businesses and internet service providers can be convicted for disseminating obscene materials for profit if they knowingly provided services to obscene websites. (article 10). These past interpretations show that  liability can extend even beyond the direct creators of illegal content to anyone who knowingly facilitates its production or dissemination .
Prosecutors alleged that Alien Chat’s developers intentionally altered their service to reliably provide sexually explicit responses, but, under the logic of the earlier interpretations, it might not even be necessary to prove that. Even knowingly facilitating a model’s use used to generate obscene materials could well be sufficient; and China’s three existing AI regulations require that service providers remain actively aware of how users engage their products.
What’s the Harm
While comparing the Alien Chat case to the Grok controversy is appealing, the situations are different in important ways, including the nature of the harm involved.
The Grok scandal primarily involves the publicly-visible generation and manipulation of images that often sexualizes real he people, sometimes with the intent to harass or humiliate them.
China’s “Alien Chat ”  case concerns mainly text-based erotic chats with purely virtual avatars, seen only by individual users and for their own gratification.
Under China’s law, the Grok situation would implicate “ cyberviolence ” law as well as normal obscenity offenses. Cyberviolence offenses have identifiable victims and more directly attributable harms. The non-criminal harassment offense in the  Public Security Administration Punishments Law  lists disseminating obscene images as one means of harassment. (article 50).
It can be harder to articulate the specific harm in more general obscenity controls.  Almost every nation has some form of obscenity law, and, like China’s, they are usually based vaguely on the protection of public morality and emphasizing the harm to minors.
In the Alien Chat case, the offending chats were solely between  [2]  individual adults and a machine, leaving some to ask what the harm is. These fenced-off chats have little chance of polluting the online ecosystem, no children were involved, nor was anyone coerced or enticed into performing to create the chats. The court seemingly focused on the broader impact of the chatbot across multiple users, rather than individual users.
The AI itself as Obscene materials
The first court to hear the case seems to have assumed that the obscene material involved were the records of users’ explicit chats. This approach explains some of the confusion in identifying the problematic content’s creators and harm: the software developers weren’t ‘present’ when the chats were created, and the societal harm only occurs when we look at the reach in terms of the software, not the individual users.
An alternative approach is to consider the chatbot itself as an interactive, immersive obscene work disseminated by the developers. After all, users do not distinguish the chats from the App, they are records of engagement with the app not works commissioned from the app. The first-instance courts’ judgment document hasn’t been released publicly, but from what is known, this approach is a better fit with the court’s overall reasoning.
Consider its emphasis on the defendants’ allegedly tweaking the model to actively guide chats towards pornographic content, regardless of user inputs. It was noted that 141 of 150 paying users sampled by investigations had obscene chats. As discussed above, this was introduced to show that the developers had knowledge of how the software was used and a role in the production of the obscene chats. What it could show even more directly is that the chatbot was altered to become an obscene work that required minimal (“press any key for porn”) engagement for a user to activate.
Generative AI is a unique technology in that it empowers users to easily make “original” works with minimal skill, cost, or effort. The accused developers here would argue that their service resembles a video camera or paintbrush – a neutral tool that could be used to create obscene or non-obscene content. If it was truly predisposed to generate obscene dialog by design, however, that argument falls apart, the user is doing little more than running the program.
The court also indicated that the correct charge was “producing” rather than “disseminating” obscene material. This may have been a way of avoiding the awkwardness of the chats being private, and not really disseminated beyond the individual user.  [3]  It may also have reflected that the service being provided intuitively feels more active than mere distribution. In either case the problem is resolved by treating the chatbot itself to be the obscene material: that work is clearly produced by defendants and, as the court highlighted, reached a large number of paying subscribers.
So why didn’t the court address the work itself as obscene material? It may be that the AI’s code doesn’t seem ‘obscene’ in itself, while the text records are traditional obscene material. The code doesn’t depict sexual acts (until activated) and isn’t blatant pornography- it requires some engagement to do that. It may be that the court hoped to avoid the thorny issue of determining when a model capable of generating both obscene and non-obscene content becomes a obscene itself: when 100% of content is obscene? 51%? This issue could probably be avoided by emphasizing intent.
Conclusion:
Defining and punishing obscenity is a thorny issue in any legal system, particularly where only consenting adults are involved. With an AI-generated chatbot the issue is slightly harder, because the offense is even more private.
China’s law on obscenity is strict. Obscene AI chatbots are a new medium that adds a few new wrinkles, but based on existing criminal law, it seems clear that creation of an explicit chatbot is within the scope of conduct that the law intends to punish and deter. The law, as clarified by the interpretations targets those who knowingly use the prurient appeal of sexually explicit materials to draw in traffic and profit.
Determining what material is ‘obscene’ is a separate and harder issue, as is whether jail time should ever result for purveying purely fictional obscene materials. China at least seems to agree that the individual users, consumers of the obscene materials, don’t need to be punished.
[1]  Cybersecurity Administration of China, National Development and Reform Commission, Ministry of Education, Ministry of Science and Technology, Ministry of Industry and Information Technology, Ministry of Public Security, and the State Administration for Radio and Television
[2]  Naturally, adult users could share chats, and one user has been separately charged for sharing a chat character he created that was also used for explicit chat purposes. See:  https://mp.weixin.qq.com/s/9ZOaPVF1l1L1WOEamDvOUw
[3]  We  recently discussed in a different context  that the letter of the law probably reached this one-to-one dissemination, but is not enforced this way in practice.

title: Did China make sexting illegal?
text:
A Chinese media report published in late December 2025 set off an online furor,  with headlines warning  that the private exchange of “indecent” images, even between consenting adults, would soon become illegal.  Foreign media  quickly picked up the story, saying that “new regulations” could soon penalize even “consensual sexting.”
The story went viral. It was also wrong.
Within days, Chinese state-media outlets and legal experts  moved quickly to correct the misinterpretation . The  original article  was taken down, but not before  multiple versions  of it had spread online. AI chatbots, trained on the initial coverage, continue to recycle the claims in an  increasingly familiar  cycle of perpetuating misinformation.
Beyond the specific legal misunderstanding, the episode underscores a broader, familiar problem shared by both China and the United States:  the rapid speed at which misinformation spreads across media ecosystems, and its endurance even after correction.
So, what actually changed in the law.
The quick answer is “very little”.
The story stemmed from recent revisions to China’s   Public Security Administration Punishments Law  (“PSAPL”), the law addressing low-level misconduct that falls short of justifying criminal charges, which took effect on January 1, 2026. Because the PSAPL allows police to directly punish citizens for minor offenses without a court trial, it is understandably of great interest to the public, and this is  not the first debate  to arise from the recent revisions.
This controversy surrounds  Article 80  of the PSAPL, which addresses two types of misconduct related to “obscenity”:
Producing, transporting, reproducing, selling, or renting    obscene materials    such as books, videos, etc., and
Disseminating    obscene information    through information networks, telephones, and other means of communication.
These offenses have existed in the law since 2005, but the new round of revisions did make a few notable changes:
Renumbering . Moving the offense from Article 68 in the old law to Article 80 in the new law (to accommodate other additions and deletions) isn’t a substantive change, but makes side-by-side comparison harder for casual readers, making it easier to believe unfamiliar language was entirely new.
Fines were increased   for the article 80 offenses and also for many other offenses throughout the law. That the length of potential detention was left unchanged for these obscenity offenses suggests that the value increases may reflect adjustments for inflation and an increased preference for financial penalties over incarceration, rather than a reevaluation of the seriousness of the offense.
A second paragraph was added calling for higher penalties where minors are involved in the obscene materials or information.  Generally, this is uncontroversial given the global emphasis on stopping sexual exploitation of minors. In 2020, China made it illegal to even possess child sexual abuse materials (CSAM) for the first time in its  Law on the Protection of Minors . Unfortunately, there is no clear enforcement mechanism for that possession, and it’s  regrettable that possession wasn’t added to this offense when revising the law.
The phrase “  computer   information systems” was shortened to simply “information systems” in the second offense.   This one-word change, eliminating “computer”, seems to be the only revision that could impact the conduct covered by the law, so it will be discussed in greater detail below.
TABLE 1: Shows the revisions in Chinese and English, with deletions highlighted in Red, additions in Green:
Where’s the Beef?
The mistaken domestic and foreign reporting seems concerned that “information networks” is a significantly broader category than “  computer   information networks,” and that this rephrasing greatly expands the  ‘dissemination’ now covered by the law. The idea is that “computer” limited the law’s application to a subset of ‘information networks’
Before considering whether that’s true, however, there are a few things to keep in mind, which might make the question less relevant:
Obscenity is not defined or expanded here . It’s clear from comparing the plain language of the original and revised versions of the law that nothing is added to alter the scope of “obscenity” in either the  obscene materials  or  obscene information  If the revisions expand the offenses at all, it can only be in terms of  how  or  where  obscene information is disseminated, as there is no change in  what  information is obscene.  [i]
In both versions of the Law,  “(computer) information networks” is just one item in a list  that also covers telephones and “other communications tools.” The rephrasing can only expand the offense if the new term (information networks) includes dissemination methods that fall outside both the old “computer information networks” and the catch-all “other communications tools.”
“Dissemination” likely already includes all digital media.  In 2004, China’s highest court and prosecutors office released  an interpretation  expanding the enforcement of obscenity crimes into the digital sphere. That interpretation made clear that even though the Criminal Law did not mention digital technologies on its face, the existing criminal charges of ‘producing, reproducing, selling, or disseminating obscene materials” were broad enough to cover sharing digital information online in chatrooms, forums, instant messaging, e-mail, websites and so forth.When the PSAPL was released one year later in 2005, its obscenity offenses were updated to align with the criminal law interpretations by expressly including the use of computer networks and other electronic means of dissemination to spread ‘obscene information”. Public security administration offenses are non-criminal, but usually directly correspond to criminal offenses and apply similar interpretations.
Neither version of the law distinguishes between   private and public dissemination of   obscene information.  This is underscored by the inclusion of “telephone” as a means of dissemination. While multi-party conference calls are no longer uncommon, “telephone” on its face naturally includes ordinary private person-to-person calls, unless they are expressly excluded.
In some situations, it makes sense not to have an exemption for consensual private chats in obscenity charges. In many jurisdictions, for example, it’s illegal to transmit CSAM and other obscene materials through, regardless of where that happens.
Use of private channels can actually increase to the harm of sexually explicit materials when they are sent to harass a non-consenting recipient, but that situation is more appropriately addressed based on the intent to harass than the obscene nature of the content sent. The PSAPL contains a separate offense for this sort of harassment at article 50(5). [ii]
Sexting has always been illegal.
The specific language can and will be further dissected below, but the discussion thus far already strongly suggests the new revisions do not meaningfully expand Article 80’s scope.
This is likely little comfort to ordinary citizens, however, because it means that  ‘sexting’ has always been illegal,  even if they didn’t know it. It also means, however, that consensual sharing of obscene images of oneself with a partner hasn’t been a focus of enforcement.
Enforcement has instead focused on the harm to public order from spreading obscene materials, making private sharing between partners a low priority. In the criminal context , successive interpretations  have consistently measured the severity of the offense by the  volume of material shared, the size of the audience  and impact , and the amount of profit gained  (with additional penalties for impact on minors).
While there is regional variation in how police exercise their discretion to give public security administration penalties, they usually closely track the logic of criminal interpretations.  Beijing ’s police standards, for example, directly refer to the volume/numbers/profits set in the criminal interpretations.
While private sharing of indecent information about oneself with direct partners has been a low priority, the law never rules out the possibility. If legislators wanted, it would not be difficult to clarify that penalties should be waived in cases of sharing images of one’s own body with a sexual partner, but thus far interpretations have not done so. Perhaps the uproar over this article will inspire one to do so.
For now, Article 20 of the PSAPL allows that penalties may be waived or reduced where the offense is minor, as in cases with a single, consenting recipient.
Police Access
A practical matter for why obscenity charges haven’t reached ‘sexting’ is that, absent a complaint or forwarding to more parties, police aren’t likely to come across it. As a general rule, Chinese police are not reviewing the millions of texts and DMs sent every day, but can do so in an open criminal investigation.  [iii]
Content moderation is generally ‘outsourced’ to the platforms. Platforms usually have the power to collect and scan messaging content in order to enforce rules against spam and illegal content  [iv]   and are required to report illegal content to the police.  （Article 34）
During a  2010 campaign to fight obscenity online , police drafted the major mobile service providers to scan text messages for pornographic and obscene messages. At the time, the  public was quite concerned  about the invasion of privacy and even afraid of accidentally violating the rules by sending dirty jokes and pictures.
The enforcement, however, targeted mainly the mass sending of texts or sending texts to harass. The consequences usually involved interrupting transmission of individual texts or limiting texting functionality, although police could disable the phones.  Automated word filter systems have sometimes interrupted innocent messaging between consenting individuals, such as when a text saying “ Let’s make a bet ” to a friend was blocked as marketing gambling.
Today’s platform enforcement is likely to follow a similar pattern.
Computer No Computer? Does it matter? (An unnecessarily deep dive)
For the reasons stated above, it doesn’t seem very worthwhile to deeply analyze whether “information systems” is a broader category than “computer information systems”. Sexting is covered by the clear language of the law in both the old and new versions of the PSAPL, but unlikely to be enforced.
For those interested, however, we can go down the rabbit hole to watch how imprecisely language has been used.
In 2021, the term “ Information Networks ” was defined expansively by the Supreme People’s Procuratorate (China’s highest prosecution body) in its  Provisions on Handling Cybercrime Cases :
“Article 61(1):  “Information networks” include the computer Internet, radio and television networks, stationery communications networks, and mobile communication networks, that have computers, television sets, landline telephones, mobile telephones, or other electronic devices as their terminals, as well as local area networks;”
That definition is really very broad. It even includes telephone communications, which are listed separately from “(Computer) Information Systems” in the PSAPL provisions on dissemination of obscene materials.  The same term could have different meanings in different legal contexts, but the Criminal Law and PSAPL are closely linked, containing violations differing mainly on their seriousness, and it’s likely that the PSAPLwas adopting this language to align the laws.
The Cybercrime Provisions explicitly apply to all crimes “committed against information networks” OR “committed using information networks.”
Within the Criminal Law itself, however, the specific crimes do not consistently use the phrase “information networks.” That phrase only first entered the text of the Law with 2015’s Criminal Law Amendment (IX) and 2020’s Amendment (XI). Both of these Amendments were released after the two Cybercrime interpretations and seem to be embracing that new terminology.
Before then, following a major overhaul of the Criminal Law in 1997, a related term “computer information systems” (计算机信息系统) (not networks) was used.  This phrase only appeared in two articles, addressing hacking offenses (entering or taking control of computer systems), destruction of computer systems, and failure to perform legally-required duties to maintain computer systems.
That term, ‘computer information systems,’ was borrowed from other legal contexts, such as the 1994   Regulations on the Protection of Computer Information Systems   ,  and likely had the same meaning:
“Computer Information System”  as used in these Regulations, refers to a human–machine system composed of computers and their related supporting equipment and facilities (including networks), which, in accordance with specific application objectives and rules, carries out the collection, processing, storage, transmission, and retrieval of information
The PSAPL uses the similar phrase, “Computer information networks”, but notice that the definition of “computer information systems” here includes the word ‘network’ as a component of systems, making it even more unclear which is the larger category.
“Computer Information Networks” isn’t defined in national law that I could identify,  but some local regulations do define it in  a manner strikingly similar to definitions of “Computer Information Systems,”only adding further clarification that Internet connectivity isn’t required.
“Computer Information Network”  refers to a  human–machine system  composed of computers and their related and supporting equipment, in accordance with certain application objectives and rules, carries out the collection, processing, storage, transmission,, and retrieval of information, including computer information systems that are not connected to the Internet (including local area networks) and computer information systems that are connected to the Internet (including via wireless access).
In 2016, the Cybersecurity Law (literally the “Network Security Law”) embraced the term “network”, but defining it largely in line with the previous definition of ‘computer information systems”.
(1) “Networks” refers to systems composed of computers or other information terminals and related equipment that follow certain rules and procedures for information collection, storage, transmission, exchange and processing.
The Cybersecurity Law and some of the earlier rules on the protection of computer information systems or networks are not focused narrowly on criminal and administrative offenses and may have different understandings of these terms. Even still it becomes increasing clear that the terminology is not as clear as one might hope.
“Computer information systems” are defined using the word “network”, and Networks are defined as systems of computers. For the purposes of law enforcement, “information systems” includes ‘computer information systems” In the Cybersecurity Law, “network” seems to be expansive, although it might
What is clear, however, is that all of these terms are meant to be expansive and inclusive. They terminology seems to be changing to include emerging and future technologies, rather than to expand the scope of the law. Yesterday’s understanding of ‘computers’ or even ‘internet’ are already obsolete in an era of bluetooth and smart toilets- but the law is meant to cover ‘communication’ and that’s clear enough.
[i]  The Public Security Administration Punishments Law does not define obscene materials or information. The Criminal Law does have a definition that is applied to the criminal offenses corresponding with PSAPL article 80, and this is applied in practice.
Article 367: 　 [Scope of Obscene Materials] “Obscene materials,” as used in this Law, refers to books, periodicals, films, videotapes, audiotapes, images, and other items that are lewd (lust-inciting) in nature and that specifically depict sexual conduct or openly promote explicit content.
Scientific works on human physiology and medical knowledge are not obscene materials.
Literary and artistic works of artistic value containing sexual content are not considered obscene materials
[ii]  In 2014, the Ministry of Public Security actually addressed a seeming contradiction between the offenses of disseminating obscene materials to harass, versus the normal obscenity charges. While these charges are distinguishable by their intent, the harassment offense required ‘sending’ material multiple times, where a single dissemination could satisfy the general obscenity charge. Some officers felt that this made the harassment charge irrelevant. The Ministry clarified that these were separate offenses, with discrete harms and subjective intent, even if similar conduct might be involved.   公安部法制局对《治安管理处罚法》相关问题的网上解答（一）  ,.
[iii]  Police generally have the power to access social media accounts etc., only after a case has been formally filed and an investigation has begun.This is not a terribly high standard, and is reviewed by other police. While I’ve never seen it, they could conceivably come across sexting while investigating another offense.  https://www.chinalawtranslate.com/en/provision-on-collection-and-review-of-digital-information-in-criminal-cases/
[iv]  Weibo’s User agreement specifically includes private messaging in content it ‘collects’ to enforce rules, but this is not necessarily the same as saying that a human reads DMs. See ( Weibo User Agreement 4.12 )

title: Reality Check: Condom Tax
text:
You’ve probably heard that China is imposing a tax on condoms to increase birth rates.
While based on recent legal changes, the story is pretty misleading. ,
Bottom Line:    Commercial birth control is  no longer tax exempt , but a government program continues  providing free contraceptives,  including condoms, pills, and IUDs.
Is there a new tax?
Yes, but with an asterisk.
Since 1994,   China’s Provisional Regulations on the Value-Added Tax  included “contraceptive drugs and devices” in its short list of exempted items.  The exempted status actually goes back even further  to at least 1984 .
On January 1, 2026, however, the Provisional Regulations were replaced by the new  Value-Added Tax Law , which did not include contraceptives in its updated list of exempted items.
So, while a new tax wasn’t introduced to specifically target contraceptives, they are now subject to the 13% VAT tax like most other goods and services.
Is the change to encourage baby-making?
No official explanation was given for ending tax-exempt status, but scholars have pointed out that the exemption seemed at odds with the current policies encouraging births.
The idea, however, isn’t that the tax status shift is intended to create a barrier to accessing contraception. Instead, the link is viewed as  creating additional revenue (an expected $93M U.S. each year) that can be used to support other programs encouraging children, such as maternity support and childcare subsidies .
The VAT Tax Law adds these types of family supports to its list of tax exemptions for the first time, but  earlier tax rules  even though they were not specifically listed in the Provisional Regulations.
While the legacy of the one-child policy has left Chinese citizens and foreign observers vigilant against government reach into reproductive choices, the tax change seems much more mundane – especially in light of ongoing programs providing free contraception.
FREE CONTRACEPTIVES!
The change in tax status for commercially available contraception is not related to long-standing and ongoing Chinese government programs providing free contraception. From what I gather, these programs, like their foreign equivalents, are used much less frequently than commercial sales, but their continued existence seems to undermine the narrative that the new tax is to discourage the use of birth control.
In 2017, shortly after the end of the One-Child policy, existing programs providing free contraception became included in the  National Basic Public Health Service Program , standardizing funding and quality controls.
The  2023 National Basic Public Services Standards , which remain in effect now, still include the provision of free contraception, and there is no reason to expect a change. The program is led by the Natinal Health Commission, and funding is divided between local and central governments.
Because the budgets include funds for publicity and education on contraception, there is a lot of information available about these programs.
A quick search on Wechat or Baidu for “国家免费提供避孕药具” 【Government provision of free contraception】will lead you to local services providing free birth control!
Below are some machine translated screen shots from recent materials online:
ABOVE: In Beijing, a variety of condom styles are offered online and can be delivered to your home or picked up at a neighborhood office. There is a limit of 40 condoms every 3 months per person!
The order screenshot from a Beijing area providers online store. The price is 0, delivery is through the post.
ABOVE: Beyond condoms, a wide range of contraceptives may be available through the programs, though there is variation in regional availability: Subdermal implants, injection, spermicides, condoms, IUDs, etc.
Sometimes you can find free contraception vending machines in residential communities, college campuses, and subway stations.
The machine near my house seems abandoned, likely in favor of more modern online options.

title: The Friends We’ve Made: An Initial Breakdown of Draft Rules for Human-like AI
text:
China’s  newly released draft rules on human-like interactive AI  mark one of the most direct regulatory responses globally to the rapid rise of anthropomorphic technologies, such as chatbots, and the growing unease over their effects on data security and users’ mental health.
The draft is ambitious. It seeks to construct an overarching framework for governing a fast-moving technology that blurs the line between tool and companion, while acknowledging the tension between encouraging innovation and containing novel risks. Article 3 frames the effort as a search for balance: promoting development while preventing abuse and maintaining control.
Yet for all its good intentions and some strong protections, the draft is frustratingly vague regarding some specific procedures, and may even risk creating new dangers in the name of protection. A notable example is requiring service providers to  profile users  based on their interactions with the AI and other available data, both to identify minors and to assess behavioral or psychological risk. These assessments can result in  reporting outside the chat, and even direct manual takeover  of the interaction. Without clear limits, this approach could deepen surveillance, expand data collection, and expose the very users the rules are meant to safeguard to new forms of harm.
I. Scope and Terminology
II. Protections
General Provider obligations
Prohibited Service Conduct
Mental Health Impact
Risk Identification
Risk Responses
Special Protections for Vulnerable Populations: Minors and Elderly
Data Security
Training Data
User Data
III. Additional Protections and Provider Responsibilities
IV. Enforcement
V. Annotated Translation Chart
I.  Scope and Terminology:
Human-like interactive AI services:  Article 2 sets the scope of the new draft by defining the covered technology. The coverage is far-reaching, clearly meant to include current chatbots and devices in which they are embedded, as well as future technologies with similar functions. The key traits are that the AI products or services:
Simulates human traits  in terms of personality, thinking, communication, and emotional interaction.
Interacts with human users  through text, images, audio, video, etc.
The definition of relevant  service providers  (article 30) clarifies that the draft covers both individuals and organizations providing such services.
Artificial Intelligence  is not a term defined in the new draft, but  previous regulations   have defined  Generative AI   as “models and relevant technologies that have the ability to generate content such as texts, images, audio, or video.”  The new drafts seemingly address a subset of that category.
Emotional Companionship Services : A few articles in the new draft single out this category of human-like interactive services, but don’t offer a clear definition. Article 18, for example, provides that exiting such a service must be easy to limit excessive dependence. Article 12 provides special requirements for when these services are provided to minors.
This term will either need to be defined or have such special requirements expanded to cover all human-like AI services.
Safety/Security:  For clarity in the discussion, it is important to note that the word 安全 （ anquan ）is used to mean both safety and security in Chinese. There is a discussion to be had regarding whether those English words reflect concepts so distinct that they should be distinguished more clearly in any language, but for now understand that both meanings are included whenever either English word is used.
II. Protections
A.   General Provider Obligations
Service providers have the primary responsibility for the security of their AI services (art. 8). They are responsible for security throughout the entire lifecycle of the products and services, from design to implementation, and including all updates and revisions.. Updates in security and functions are to be concurrently updated, so that there is no lag in protection for new features. (article 9)
The ‘security’ mentioned here is wide-ranging and touches on other laws; it includes: data security, pre-publication content reviews, cybersecurity, personal information protection, and fraud prevention.  (Art. 8).
Special security assessments , focusing on user traits, and the implementation of safety features must be conducted and reported to provincial authorities when services introduce new functions or cause major changes to existing functions, and achieve high user volumes (1M or more registered/ 100,000 or more monthly active users). Security assessments may also be triggered by a possible impact on national security or the public interest, or undefined security shortfalls. (articles 21,22).
B.   Prohibited Service Conduct
Article 7  contains a non-exclusive list of prohibited conduct for human-like interactive AIs, laying a fundamental bottom line on AI conduct.
The first several items in the list relate to  content restrictions , generally incorporating China’s  broader online content controls  into this new context. Content that is punishable under existing laws must not be generated or disseminated by the AI services.
This includes content endangering national security, harming the national honor and interests, conducting illegal religious activities, disrupting order, and undermining ethnic unity, as well as promoting gambling, obscenity, encouraging crimes, and conduct that impacts others’ rights, such as defamation. (art. 7 (1)-(3)).
While there are plenty of legitimate criticisms of China’s content control regime, this type of restriction is not new, and has been  consistent across recent AI regulations , so it is not the focus of discussion here.
The article 7 list, however, also includes several vague items more specific to the human-like AIs. These require that AIs not make  false promises  that impact user conduct, or include services that  harm social relations . They require that suicide not be encouraged, glamorized, and forbid verbal violence and emotional manipulation, as well as algorithmic manipulation, misleading information, and  emotional traps .
These all sound like bad things that should be avoided, but unless they are more clearly defined, it will be difficult for service providers to  understand  and implement the prohibitions.
The final article 7 prohibition relates to  data security,  requiring that the AI not deceptively acquire information related to state secrets or sensitive information. While these categories of information sound familiar from China’s  Law on State Secrets  and  Personal Information Protection Law , it isn’t entirely clear what conduct is prohibited here, if any, beyond those laws’ requirements.
C.   Mental Health Impact
The user impacts that the draft seems most concerned with include  excessive dependency   or addiction  to the services and  extreme emotional responses or mental health emergencies,  such as risks of suicide and self-harm (but notably not harm to others). (art.9)
To  prevent these risks , a number of clear preventative and response measures are required.  For example, Article 16 requires c onspicuous notices that the chat is with an AI and not a human being.  More dynamic reminders, such as pop-up window alerts, are required when users first begin using a service or newly log-in. Similarly, reminders to take a break must also be displayed every 2 hours. (art.  17)
The risks involved, however, are not limited to threats to health, and include even  risks to property . (article 11,13). This might envision risks of a user losing assets to fraud or destruction, but feels distinct enough that future revisions should consider separating out responses to this less critical risk, especially given the consequences of a risk being identified.
Risk Identification
As mentioned above, the draft requires that providers have the capacity to identify user statuses- specifically, assessing extreme emotions and potential undue dependence, and intervening when they are detected.  (art. 11)
The draft does not specify whether these determinations should be made through human review or automated systems, yet either approach raises serious concerns. Most users do not expect their interactions to be reviewed by human operators for any purpose, and permitting such review would constitute a profound intrusion into user privacy. Although the draft explicitly requires that privacy be protected during this identification process, it offers no guidance on how that goal is to be achieved in practice.
If the identification mechanism is automated, privacy is more easily maintained, but it requires reliance on the AI service that may be the source of the harm. Such reliance invites both false positives and false negatives: cases in which the system mistakenly flags benign behavior as high-risk, or, more troublingly, fails to detect genuine risks altogether.
Because the draft requires provider action and invasive interruptions in user experience when risks are detected, more detail is needed for the risk identification procedures.
One possibility would be for AI to conduct an initial assessment and pass potential risks to a professional response team after anonymizing the data. This would allow human experts to consider chat content without learning the users’ identity, before making a decision on whether a further response is required. The draft does require service providers to have relevant staff available. (art. 9).
Not all service providers will have the capacity to establish such systems, and this general procedure does not fully eliminate risks, but it is worth exploring.
Risk Responses
Different levels of risk call for different responses, further underscoring the need for clarity about the required precision and standards in risk identification.
Where a “ tendency”  towards addiction or dependency is detected, for example, pop-up windows and other more aggressive means are to be used to remind users that they are chatting with a non-human, etc. (art. 16).
In crisis situations, such as users’ extreme emotional states, clear expressions of intent to harm themselves, the response can be more drastic. (art. 11p3). First, the service is to offer users prepared template responses offering comfort and encouraging the user to seek help. This may seem familiar from US products that quickly provide information on suicide hotlines, in response to some user prompts.
Second , a human being will take over the chat . The instinct towards crisis intervention is clearly well-intentioned, but the sudden appearance of a human operator in what was understood to be a private, automated discussion of sensitive emotional content could aggravate the situation rather than de-escalate it. It may even be viewed by users as a betrayal of confidence.
Even the existence of a mechanism for human operators to enter the chat might make users wary about the services, fearing abuse. For this type of intervention to be successful, clear notice of the standards and conditions for such an intervention should be announced to users and service providers in advance.
There will also need to be rules for how and when an intervening human operator announces their presence to the user.
Special Protections for Vulnerable Populations: Minors and the Elderly
The draft has special rules for the protection of minors and the elderly.
First, companionship for  elderly  users is specifically listed as a potential future usage scenario for AIs (art. 6); so it is appropriate to consider whether that population is uniquely vulnerable.
That said, the elderly are not children, and age should not be taken as a proxy for mental capacity. Having special requirements for seniors, as opposed to offering them special accommodations and assistance, is not always appropriate.
Consider Article 13’s call for elderly users to  provide emergency contact persons who will be contacted as part of crisis response .  That option should be available to all adult users, not just the elderly, and should not be forced on anyone with full mental capacity.
Similarly, a special  prohibition on providing elderly users with services imitating their family members  or other specified individuals, presumably to avoid fraud or confusion, could be presented as an option to all competent users.
Where users lack full mental capacity, due to age, illness, or any other reason, there are legal procedures for appointing a guardian. And guardians can be consulted in those circumstances. Otherwise, special barriers should not be placed in any user’s path.
Minors  ,  on the other hand, lack full capacity by law. China has already built an extensive system of special legal protections for minors online [Previews], much of which is referenced in the draft.
Article 12, for example, calls for establishing Minors Modes for the AI services. Existing  guidelines on minors modes  for networked products call for mutual recognition across different devices, software, and platforms, so that guardians can globally activate minors mode protections with a single click.
Such rules empower guardians, giving them final decision-making authority, but set default standards for usage times and permissions based on different age brackets.
Minors Mode rules in the new draft continue to empower guardians, but make no distinctions for different-aged minors. This is in contrast with some current practices of some companies that suggest children below a certain age not use human-like AI at all, and have increased oversight for older children. ( See e.g.  OPEN AI FAQ ).
The rules require that guardians be granted authority to toggle Minors Mode, and have settings for usage time limits, and in-service ‘reality checks’ (reminders that the AI is a non-human). Their consent is required for collecting minors’ data and providing it to third parties (PIPL), and they may exercise other user rights on behalf of the minor, such as requesting the deletion of information collected in interactions. (art. 14).
For  ‘emotional companionship’ services , guardians have additional authority. First, they must expressly agree to the services. Then they must be able to receive real-time risk notifications and summaries of the minors’ usage, to block specific characters and roles in the interactions, and limit the minors’ usage time, adding funds, and spending.
All of these concepts, however, remain vague and need refinement to be operational. What standard determines when real-time risk alerts are issued? Is the content of chats included in summaries of minors’ usage, or only duration?
Identification of Minors
Outside of China, determining who is a minor remains a contentious issue, as it requires all users, including adults, to verify their age. Increasingly, this means uploading identification documents, creating an increased risk of harmful data breaches, and making true anonymity online impossible.
China’s internet already operates on a real-name registration system, effectively requiring all users to link their national ID to most online accounts. Even this drastic measure has not proved fully effective in blocking minors’ access to improper online content; however, as kids have learned to borrow parental accounts (with and without permission), rent other adults’ accounts, and fool ID systems. ( see eg: https://finance.sina.com.cn/tech/digi/2025-12-18/doc-inhcfepi6674554.shtml )
The draft human-like AI rules go further, requiring the AI services to have the capacity to identify minors. (art. 12). Such algorithmic detection of minors was hinted at previously in the Ministry of Culture and Tourism’s    Opinions on Strengthening the Protection of Minors in the Online Cultural Market   (at 4), but this seems to be the first proposal to fully implement the idea.
Profiling user conduct in service interaction clearly impacts the privacy of all users. In recognition of this, a draft version of rules on Generative AI once precluded profiling based on user input- but that requirement was not ultimately adopted.  (Art. 11).
The human-like AI draft says that privacy must be protected in profiling for minors, but, as with profiling to identify risky behaviors, it is difficult to know how this will occur without detailed procedures and standards.
Moreover, the consequence of being identified as a suspected minor is being automatically placed in Minor’s Mode. Appeals process for restoration of full services are also required, but again, no clear standards are provided.
D. Data Security
Training Data :  All training data, for pretraining or optimization, is regulated by Article 10 of the draft. The requirements overlap somewhat with previous provisions on  Generative AI training data  (art.7) in requiring legal and diverse sources, but continue primarily by emphasizing safety.
Training data should be cleaned and labeled to increase transparency and avoid contamination or tampering, if synthesized data is recycled as training data, its safety must be assessed.  Negative sampling and adversarial sampling techniques must be used to avoid unintended or inappropriate content associations.
Naturally, training data must also conform to content laws, and reflect the ‘core socialist values’ and Chinese culture- something no doubt considered a safety issue as well.
Importantly, interaction records and sensitive personal information (identifying information that would likely cause harm if disclosed) must also not be used in training models without clear legal authorization or user consent. (art. 15)
User Data
While the provisions of the  Personal Information Protection Law  and  Data Security Law  contain the basic framework for collecting, sharing, and processing user data, the Draft makes a few key provisions as well.
Article 15, for example, calls for annual compliance audits of the handling of minors’ information, a rule in accordance with article 37 of the  Regulation for the Online Protection of Minors.
Providers must use tools to  protect the content of users’ interactions  with AI, including through encryption, security audits, and access controls. (art.14). These protections are similar to privacy requirements for user inputs in  other AI rules  (see art. 11), but suffer from a similar defect- requirements that they monitor and report interactions deemed unlawful. (art. 23, and profiling requirements above).
Users, or their guardians when applicable, may request that data be deleted at any time. (art. 14).
III. Additional Protections and Provider Responsibilities
Sandboxes  Article 27 calls for the establishment of sandbox platforms for testing security and innovations.
Complaint Mechanisms-  Procedures for providers’ acceptance and addressing of complaints are in Article 22.
Algorithm Filings:   Required by Article 25 In accordance with  existing rules .
IV.Enforcement (Articles 26, 28, 29)
Provincial-level internet information departments (the organs of the Cybersecurity Administration of China) are to conduct annual document-based audits of service providers’ compliance. Where providers have not adequately performed required security assessments, they may be asked to do so again, and the departments may carry out on-site inspections.
These provincial-level departments and other regulatory departments can call in the legal representatives and other leadership of service providers where they find security risks or security incidents. (art. 28).  At these meetings, they can guide corrections of and action to eliminate threats.
Providers are to cooperate and  provide necessary support and assistance  for departments carrying out inspections in accordance with law. Previously, similar language requiring cooperation in  Cybersecurity Law  article 28, has been viewed abroad as meaning that companies are required to act as agents of Chinese authorities. Here it seems clear that the requirement is limited to assisting in the enforcement of these measures, but this does include rules related to national security threats, so I suspect those concerns will continue regarding human-like AIs.
Generally,  penalties  are left for other laws to define, but article 29 does allow for administrative penalties where no other laws apply. Violations of these measures are generally to be met with warnings, criticism, or orders to make corrections; but services may be ordered paused where corrections aren’t made or there are other serious circumstances.
V. Annotated Chart

title: Suitable for all audiences? [Part I]
SPC Cases on Domestic Violence
text:
On November 21, 2025, The Supreme People’s Court  (SPC)  released a series of cases illustrating principles in cases related to domestic violence . These cases are intended to serve as both public legal education and guidance to lower courts.  The release shows a generally positive direction, and was interesting in that the cases were presented as showing compliance with international legal norms.
Case 1: Psychological Abuse Leading To Suicide
FACTS:
Couple lived together from 9/2018 to 10/2019.
1/2019, M became increasingly argumentative about F’s sexual history, constantly and cruelly berating her.
6/2019, F cut her wrists after an argument.
8/2019, F attempted suicide by taking pills, but was revived.
10/2019, F fled to a hotel after an argument with M, ordered pills online, and ended her own life.
Verdict:  Sentenced to 3 years, 2 months prison.
Legal Principles:
Cohabitating partners are “family members” covered by the crime of familial abuse.   Case materials discuss that romantic cohabitation includes the unique features of family violence: occuring in private, difficult to remove oneself and avoid escalation.
Article 260:  [Criminal Abuse]  Where the circumstances are heinous, abuse of  family members  is to be given a sentence of up to two years imprisonment, criminal detention, or controlled release.
Violations of the crime in the preceding paragraph that cause serious injury or death to the defendant are given a sentence of between 2 and 7 years imprisonment.
The crime in the first paragraph is handled only upon complaint, except where the victim lacks the capacity to make a complaint, or is unable to make a complaint due to coercion or intimidation.
The  Domestic Violence Law  explicitly extends to cover cohabitation, but this case demonstrates how that should impact the application of the  Criminal Law  in domestic violence cases.
2.  Sustained humiliation and degradation, causing psychological torment, can be addressed as Criminal Familial ABUSE when the circumstances are vile.  This follows an interpretation of domestic violence offenses released by the SPC in 2015, prior to the Domestic Violence Law, stating:
Where the defendant does not subjectively have the intent to encroach upon the health of the victim or take the life of the victim, but rather seeks the victims’ physical or psychological suffering, and commits acts of abuse against them over a long period or on several occasions, gradually causing physical harm to the victim, negligently causing the victim to suffer serious injury or death; or where the victim harms or kills themselves because they are unable to stand the abuse, causing serious injury or death; it is within the scope of “causing serious harm or death to the victim” as provided for in paragraph 2 of Criminal Law article 260, and shall be convicted and punished as the crime of abuse.
3. Causation between the abuse and suicide is established by the abuser’s knowledge of the victim’s fragile state.
Case 2: Familial Rape
FACTS:
In 7/2022, Wu moved in with her mother and stepfather.
The stepfather was abusive to the mother and told Wu he had served time for homicide.
October 5, 2022- The mother fled the house.
The stepfather then recorded a video of himself having sex with Wu.
Wu wrote her mother, who called the police.
VERDICT:  Stepfather sentenced to 4 years 6 months.
Legal Principles:
Lack of resistance cannot be taken as consent in familial rape.  The case explanation notes that domestic violence is typified by control, and given the uniquely vulnerable and dependent position of the victim, lack of resistance or passive compliance is consistent with fear and cannot be viewed as voluntary consent.
Expert testimony on minors’ psychological states is permissible.  When cases involve specialised knowledge, experts can be invited- here addressing trauma to minors, neural development, etc., helping the court understand the response without bias.   Articles 146 and 197 of the  Criminal Procedure Law  allow for persons with expert subject area knowledge to inform the court about related issues.
Case 3: Delayed Reporting of Domestic Violence
F and M romantically cohabitated
5/2023, they fight at F’s workplace, with M stabbing F, threatening to kill her, and then stabbing himself.
F’s sister intervened, helped F get medical attention. The couple separates.
8/2023, M returns to Fs work, and threatens her jealously.
F calls the police, reporting previous stabbing, and M is arrested.
VERDICT:  M is sentenced to 6 years for attempted murder.
Legal Principles:
The pattern of domestic violence should be considered in determining whether the offender had the intent to kill.  Domestic violence is based on control, and when that control is threatened, the abuser may try to destroy the victim. A series of threatening texts shows that Xu feared losing control, and that this was his motive. He used very deadly means and attempted to kill himself, showing his intent was death, not injury.
Delayed reporting does not undermine credibility of the accusation.  In Domestic violence cases, victims often believe that the offender will reform and avoid reporting. Here she waited for a new threat to report, but this shouldn’t impact credibility.
CASE 4:  Evaluating Minor’s Accounts of Abuse
FACTS:
M lived with Wang’s Mother, F, since 2021.
In 2024, Wang told an aunt that M had raped her, and the aunt reported this to the police.
On May 19, police questioned Wang who recounted rapes and molestation.
On July 2, at F’s request, the police spoke with Wang again, who recanted, saying she only sought to make her mother leave M.
VERDICT:   Court believed prior 2 accounts, sentencing M to 15 years for rape and indecency.
LEGAL PRINCIPLES:
Statements from minors that contain a level of detail beyond what a non-participant would know are generally to be accepted.  Here, the level of detail, described in age-appropriate language and fashion, made it unlikely the victim was being induced, coerced, or making a false accusation. Reasons for recanting should be scrutinised, including likely intervention by the mother here.
Corroborating evidence is key to establishing the case . Chat logs with M on victims phone showed improper content; other witnesses’ verified that child had made statements consistent with accusations.
Case 5: Personal Protection Order
FACTS:
Couple married in 2008 and had a son.
During an argument, M threatened to hurt himself and pushed F when she tried to stop him.
F called the police who helped her apply for a  protection order  online. W/in 20 minutes, the court found there was Domestic Violence and issued the order, serving it to both parties.
Despite concerted education and enforcement, violence continued, and F was granted shelter while a divorce was mediated.
Legal Principles:
Threats of self-harm can be psychological violence against others.  The heart of DV is control, and threatening to harm oneself can be used to coerce a partner and constitute psychological violence.
Collaborative enforcement-  police, local organisations, health care providers, and courts all provide services and help establish a safety structure and mechanisms for issuing and enforcing a protection order.
CASE 6: Corporal Discipline can be Domestic Violence
FACTS:
F and M divorced with one daughter (D).
M was aggressive and sometimes physically punished D.
At 10 years old, he hit her mouth with a slipper, causing bleeding, and F brought her to the police
At 11 M whipped D with a belt, and again F took her to the police.
D told police that she was being hit and that she wanted to live with her mother. She was diagnosed with depression and became unable to attend school.
F sued for custody, arguing that the abuse had led to depression.
Verdict: F granted Custody
Legal Principles:
Violent discipline can be domestic violence.  Frequent physical and verbal violence violates Article 2 of the Domestic Violence Law. Pang harmed his daughter and ignored the psychological harm it was causing.
Custody decisions should consider domestic violence.  Violence poses a physical and psychological threat and is not in the best interests of the child, which is the standard for determining custody.
CASE 7
FACTS:
F and M married in 1993 and had 4 children, now grown.
F was stay at home parent and homemaker, and M was verbally and physically abusive.
In September 2019 violent abuse caused guards to intervene, and hospital records document bruises.
In May 2022, F sued for divorce with division of property, AND sought compensation for labor and damages.
LEGAL PRINCIPLES:
Fair division of property can protect women from further abuse/harassment.  Fair can mean considering practical realities as well as values, ensuring a stable livelihood. Here, the court divided storefront rental properties not only by value, but also by location, to allow F to continue renting them with minimal interaction with M.
The value of domestic labor can be evaluated at divorce.  100,000 yuan in compensation given in addition to division of property.
Case 8: Abuse can be grounds for custody reappraisal, but self-help is not proper remedy.
FACTS:
M and F married in 2022 despite a history of repeated, violent domestic violence, and had a child in the same year.
F sought help repeatedly during and after pregnancy from Womens Federation and police, and the police issued a  warning letter .
In April 2023, the couple divorced, agreeing that M would raise the child until age 4, at which point they would revisit custory.
6 months in, F discovered that M and his family were not protecting child, and removed her.
M asked court to enjoin F from infringing his custody, and the court did so.
F sued to change custody, and ultimately succeeded after presenting evidence of domestic violence.
Legal Principles:
History of domestic violence is a factor in establishing or changing child custody.  F was able to 1)explain that she had initially relinquished custody only to expedite the divorce, 2) present evidence of abuse by M, and 3) demonstrate M’s current financial instability etc.
Injunctions are appropriate where a non-custodial parent removes a child from the custodial parent’s care.  When the non-custodial parent believes the child is endangered, by the custodial parent’s gambling, drinking, or other abuse and neglect, the correct remedy is to sue for custody, not merely remove the child.
Applicable Law:
Interpretation of the Civil Code, Marriage and Family Section (2)
Article 12:  Where either the father or mother, or a person such as a close relative, takes or hides minor children, and the other party applies to the people’s court for a personal protection order, or applies for an injunction against harms to personality rights in accordance with Civil Code article 997, the people’s court is to support it.
Where the party taking or hiding minor children argues that there was a reasonable grounds for them to do so in that the other party had situations such as gambling, drug use, or domestic violence that seriously harmed the minor children’s lawful rights and interests, the people’s court shall notify them to resolve it through channels such as for revocation of guardianship rights, suspension of visitation, or changing childcare relationships. Where parties do not provide evidence in support of the arguments above and fail to submit the request in a reasonable time, the people’s court is to handle it in accordance with the preceding paragraph.

title: Quicktake: Large Online Platform Rules
text:
New  draft provisions  released by the CAC and MPS propose special Personal Information Protection requirements for Large Online Platforms (LOPs) established or operating in China.
The draft increases security requirements and responsibility for LOPs, treating protection of personal information as equivalent to a national security threat. Areas of specific interest include:
Personal Information Protection Officers
Data Centers and localization.
Data Portability
Compliance Audits
Scope of Coverage: CAC designation of LOPs
Covered LOPs will be designated by the CAC, in conjunction with MPS and other Relevant departments, which will release and maintain a directory of affected platforms. (Article 3).
The designation will be made primarily by  considering  the following factors, but these factors alone do not automatically trigger coverage:
User-base:  50 million or more registered users, or 10 million monthly-active users;
Service scope:  Importance or diversity of services provided,
Impact : Potential impact of a leak to national security, economy, or people’s lives.
Other factors  that the CAC and MPS provide.
Personal Information Protection Officers (个人信息保护负责人)
Background :
The concept of designated Personal Information Protection Officers (PIPOs) was formally introduced in Chinese practice in the 2020 Personal Information Security Specifications (GB/T 35273-2020), a non-binding national standard released prior to the Personal Information Protection Law.
The standard’s recommendation was that personal information handlers should designate a PIPO if they had 200 or more employees [a measure of capacity], handled 1 million or more individuals’ personal information, or 100,000 or more people’s sensitive personal information [a measure of impact]. (11.1(c)).  An extensive list of PIPO duties was provided in the standard, including wide-reaching oversight, planning, policy drafting, and auditing, foreshadowed the functions that have gradually been mandated by later authority.
In 2021, the  Personal Information Protection law  codified the PIPO role but did not set a specific standard for when designating a PIPO became mandatory, explicitly leaving this to a future CAC determination (Art. 52).  That clarification came in 2025’s  Measures on Personal Information Protection Compliance Audits , which required those handling 1 million or more persons’ personal information to designate a PIPO responsible for compliance audits. Months later, a  CAC announcement  further required that data handlers who met the 1 M mark submit information on their PIPO’s to a centralized registry.
Proposed Heightened Requirements for LOPs:
The new draft does not disturb the existing PIPO requirement for information handlers handling 1million or more individuals’ information, but adds additional requirements and duties for those designated as LOPs.
PIPO Qualifications for LOPs:
Must be a member of the platform provider’s management teams. *
Must be a Chinese citizen without long-term or permanent foreign resident status abroad. **
Must have 5 years experience and professional knowledge related to PI protections.
*The language of the management level requirement(管理层成员) is the same as that used in the  Regulations on Online Data Security Management’s requirement that handlers of “important data” designate a data security officer. (Article 30) The draft LOP provisions further clarify that the PIPO may concurrently serve as the data security officer.  (article 5). The LOP draft goes further, however, in requiring that the PIPO have veto power over personal information handling matters.
** The citizenship   and   no foreign residency requirement is incredibly strict. Even recent revisions to the Regulations on the Implementation of the State Secrets Law rejected a   draft proposal  (article 45(3) to include such a requirement for those engaged with state secrets,  ultimately opting to only to require citizenship . (Article 47)  Other authority, however,  including on the design and maintenance of computer systems for state secrets , do have a “no foreign residency” requirement for high-ranking management and direct practitioners. (article 12(3)).
LOP PIPO Duties:
Most of the duties are intuitive or familiar from the 2020 standards mentioned above. They largely include drafting and implementing internal measures for protecting personal information, and liaising with relevant authorities.  The notable exception is the requirement that the PIPO have veto authority regarding personal information decisions.
Duties:
Guide compliance, implement government requirements, and coordinate with the government.
Participate in making decisions related to PI handling,  including veto power;
Oversee handling activities and use of protective measures;
Report security risks and illegal conduct to the CAC (MPS if constitutes a crime or violation);
Creating special rules for the protection of minors’ personal information.
Additionally, the PIPO is to lead PI protection work, such as:
Drafting personal information protection management systems, operating procedures, and emergency response plans
Determining operational authority
Conducting security management
Risk monitoring, risk assessment, compliance audits. impact assessments, emergency response drills, publicity, education, and training,
Make rules for in-platform businesses’ handling of personal information
Identify persons responsible for minors’ personal information
Address complaints
Publish an annual report on personal information protection.
Data Centers and localization.
Data localization requirements for personal information are nothing new, but the LOP rules go further in specifically addressing the domestic data centers that will store such data.
Background of data localization
2017- Cybersecurity Law  Article 37    (now 39)  requires that Personal information and other Important data collected in China by  Critical Information Infrastructure operators  must be stored locally. Security assessments are required before exporting such data.
Critical information infrastructure  generally includes utilities, defense, and other systems for which the destruction, loss of function, or data leakage might seriously endanger national security, national welfare, and the people’s livelihood, or the public interest.
2021 PIPL    Article 40  expanded the data localization requirement for personal information to also include any personal information handlers that handle a quantity of personal information set by the CAC. The specific threshold was deferred for later decision. As above, security assessments are required for exports.
2022’s    Measures for the Security Assessment of Data Exports    Article 4,  effectively set the standards for when personal information handlers would be required to localize data, including those who handle 1 M or more person’s personal information, or who export 100,000 persons personal information, or 10,000 persons’ sensitive personal information in a given calendar year. 10 data centers- basic requirements
Data Center Requirements
Articles 10-13 of the LOP rules establish requirements for data centers.
LOPs must store personal information in data centers that they directly control or by retaining the services of a third party, but all data centers must meet the following requirements:
are established within the PRC
The primary responsible person is a PRC citizen and does not have foreign permanent overseas residency or long-term residency permits;*
comply with relevant national security standards.
*As discussed above in relation to PIPOs, the requirement that the person in charge be a citizen with no foreign residency is an exceedingly protective stance,  elevating personal information protection to a national security issue.*
The data centers themselves, independent of the LOP provider, are obligated to:
Establish internal personal information management systems and operating procedures;
Report any potential security flaws or vulnerabilities to the relevant authorities and the PIPO of affected LOPs, and take measures to remedy them.
Immediately report security incidents to the PIPO of affected LOPs, initiate emergency response to resolve the problem and mitigate harm, and report to the relevant authorities.
Promptly implement personal information protection requirements as required by the relevant authorities. （Article 11）
Where the data center is a retained third party, they must have a contract that provides that they will:
Complete the basic data center duties provided above;
Perform personal information protection obligations, provide safe, stable, and continuous services, and accept the oversight of the LOP’s PIPO etc.;
Facilitate the LOPs handling of personal information;
Assist in security management over personal information handling activities.
Article 13 additionally requires that information on the Data Centers be submitted to the CAC and other relevant authorities, including on their:
management team and management structure,
personal information protection systems security and measures employed,
the text of contracts with LOPs
Data Portability
PIPL article 45  provided individual network users’ general right to data portability, allowing that they could request information handlers to transfer their information to other information handlers. But required that unspecified CAC conditions must be met.
Article 14  of the provisions seems to set those conditions, at least as to LOPs.
LOPS must transfer the personal information in a universal, machine-readable format within 30 working days after receiving an individual’s request, and notify the individual of the results by email, phone, text message, etc. An additional 30 day extension is possible where there are complications, but this must be explained to the individual, as must any other impediments.
LOPs are encouraged to use technical measures such as encryption and programming interfaces to facilitate data transfers. There is normally to be no charge for transferring data, but necessary costs may be billed where individuals repeatedly transfer their personal information.
Compliance Audits
The provisions on conducting risk assessments, compliance audits, and so forth, largely accord with the  Measures on the Management of Personal Information Protection Compliance Audits  (Audit Measures) released earlier this year.  LOPs are allowed to carry out these security reviews themselves, or by retaining a third-party institutions registered in China.
The Audit Measures provided three circumstances where an information handler could be required to use an outside auditor. The LOP rules add an additional situation for where there have previously been legal violations, as well as a catch-all discretionary clause:
1) The personal information handling activities seriously impact individual rights and interests, seriously lack security measures, etc;
(2) There have been multiple violations of laws and regulations such as the illegal export of personal information;
(3) The personal information handling activities might infringe the rights and interests of a large number of individuals;
(4) leaked, altered, lost, or damaged 1 million or more people’s personal information, or 100,000 or more people’s personal information to be;
(5) Other circumstances provided by laws, regulations, or relevant competent authorities.
(Article 17)
