title: Quicktake: Types and Levels of Cybersecurity Incidents
text:
On September 11, China’s Cybersecurity Administration released more d etailed procedures for reporting cybersecurity incidents,  building on the reporting requirements in Article 25 of the  Cybersecurity Law .
The new Measures call for differentiated responses based on the incident’s severity level, and were accompanied by  Guidelines  for grading incidents as ordinary, relatively major, major, or especially major.
In addition to these general requirements, the Guidelines include several typical scenarios that will ordinarily constitute each incident level that requires reporting. Ordinary cybersecurity incidents that do not reach these standards are exempt from reporting.
Interestingly, the hacking attacks on certain key types of websites addressed in lines 1 and 6 above are separated into distinct categories based on their differing harms. 1, above, is related to blocking access while 6 involves spreading illegal and harmful information. For both types of attack, the nature of the targeted site is relevant to determining the severity, and blocking attacks are measured by its duration, but the spread of illegal information is evaluated by the range of persons with access to it.
While the Guidelines only require reporting of illegal and harmful content as a “cybersecurity incident” when it results from an attack on key sites (an intuitive cybersecurity concern), earlier national standards on which the Guidelines were based actually classified the spread of illegal content as a type of cybersecurity incident in itself.
Information Content Security Incidents are one of 10 types of cybersecurity incident contained in the  Information Security Technology—Guidelines for the Classification and Grading of Cybersecurity Incidents (GB/T 20986-2023)  The categories of content included are typical of other  content restrictions  and include advocacy of terrorism or extremism, opposing the state and party leadership, pornography, etc. Interenstingly, the severity of the offenses in the standards were not determined by the range it spread- but focused more on the seriousness of the content itself.
Those standards were recommendations only and not binding, but they hint at a more holistic understanding of cybersecurity that includes content regulation as well as technical attacks on operations. Whether the new Guidelines intentionally abandoned this approach to distinguish content regulation for cybsercurity concerns remains to be seen.

title: Inability vs. Unwillingness to Satisfy Judgments: Example Cases for the Court Judgment Defaulter List
text:
The Judgment Defaulter List
The courts’ “ judgment defaulter list ” is a unique part of China’s social credit system.
Most social credit blacklists are managed by regulatory agencies and include entities that seriously violate the laws and regulations enforced by that agency. Penalties for being blacklisted must be based on those same laws, and agencies also share blacklist information with the public and other agencies, which may consider the violations in other contexts, such as permitting or commercial transactions.
Entry into the court’s defaulter list, however, is primarily limited to those who have an effective legal judgment against them but refuse to satisfy it despite having the ability to do so. Based loosely on the courts’ power to preserve assets to satisfy judgments, penalties under the defaulter list include limits on high spending. The restrictions on air travel, fancy trains, private schooling, etc., which are frequently associated with social credit in Western media, are almost entirely restricted to the court judgment defaulter list.
With nearly 2.5 million entries made to the list in 2024 (down 23.4% from 2023), the judgment defaulter list is already a critical enforcement tool for the courts. It is so distinct from other social credit blacklists, however, that it is probably best to understand the defaulter list solely as a court enforcement mechanism rather than thinking of it as ‘social credit’ at all.
Cases on Distinguishing the “Untrustworthy” from the “Unable”
The court should only blacklist judgment defaulters who have the capacity to fulfill judgments against them, but still refuse to do so. There has been minimal attention, however, to the processes for establishing whether a party has the ability to satisfy a judgment.
The  Supreme People’s Court’s recent example cases on distinguishing ‘inability’  from ‘refusal’ to fulfil judgments  seemed set to finally address this issue.  Rather than detailing procedures for considering parties’ actual financial situations, however, the cases emphasize a more holistic, discretionary, ad hoc approach, urging judges to consider both the defaulters’ assets and attitudes when planning enforcement. The primary focus seems to be on ensuring that penalties for default do not destroy otherwise viable businesses.
Taken together, the main messages of the cases can be summarized as:
Stop mechanical application   of the judgment defaulter list in all cases, and make real inquiries into the specific reasons for default, and ensure that enforcement measures correspond to actual conditions.
Seek sustainable outcomes   for all parties:
Recognize that the goal is satisfying the judgment, not punishment, and employ measures that allow businesses to survive to acquire assets sufficient to fulfill their obligations.
Acknowledge the broader impact of a company failing, including on its customers and the industry, and encourage resolutions that protect all parties.
Consider the defaulter’s subjective malice   or good intentions, employing penalties for truly intentional harms.
Analysis
This broader, more discretionary approach to the judgment defaulter list is certainly better than blanket application but raises a number of issues.
First, is courts’ capacity to assess the viability of businesses.  In several of the cases, judges determine that despite a party’s temporary financial difficulty, it remains a profitable business that will eventually be able to pay its debts. This leads to allowing a ‘grace period’, avoiding listing as a judgment defaulter for 1-3 months, so that the business can function normally and earn money.
It’s unclear that this is something that the courts have the talent, knowledge, and resources to properly assess. In the context of criminal enforcement, a similar effort to encourage business compliance without causing economic harm by deferring prosecutions employs teams of  third-party experts to monitor ongoing compliance . A similar use of outside experts might be helpful here.
Second, is the impact on non-parties. In the example cases, the courts frequently seek the consent of the party seeking enforcement before granting the debtor a grace period for entry on the defaulter list, but what about the rights of other parties? For example, in case 4, the purpose of allowing a grace period is expressly to avoid discouraging new investment that will keep the business afloat. Hiding the default from prospective new investors seems entirely at odds with the goals of the social credit system and of sharing default information.
Third, is fairness. The holistic approach to applying the defaulter list is also a very individualized approach. While consideration of specific circumstances is important, it can also lead to very uneven implementation. Beyond individual judge’s subjective assessments of the parties’ intent and business viability, look at case 4, where the court considers the negative impact that blacklisting could cause to to the defaulter’s customers. Is it fair that a bigger business should be given greater latitude in satisfying court judgments, making it too big to default?
The Cases
Case No.  Case Name  Summary of Facts  Key Concepts
1.  Chen v. Di (Loan Debt)  Di was found to owe Chen 125,000 RMB and was placed on the Judgment Defaulter List. He developed bladder cancer, and asked to court to suspend his listing, to facilitate his seeking treatment. Chen consented and the court suspended the listing,  reminding Di to report and resume payment when able.
When Di's condition stabilized, they did not report to the court, but travelled to Hong Kong and Macao to gamble. This was reported to the court, which sent officers to meet  Di at the airport on their return. He was found in possession of expensive items and cash, and had received 3M RMB using someone else's Wechat account.
They quickly satisfied the judgment, but the court also sent the case to the police to pursue charges of evading a court judgment.  Subjective Malice
2.  Jiangxi Tech Co. v. Shanghai Car Company et. al. (sales contract dispute)  Despite promises to pay as soon as able, the defendant still failed to satisfy the judgment against it, and also had other labor dispute judgments against them.
On inquiry, the court learned that penalties might impact the company's current financing plans- and the since the business was viable, suspending penalties would increase chance of the judgment being enforced.  To ensure compliance, the court sought a guarantee and banned the legal representative from leaving the country.  At the same time, the court facilitated communication between the parties.
After financing, the judgment was successfully satisfied.   Stop Mechanical Application, Sustainable Outcomes
3  Jiang family vs. Li (Car Accident)  Li struck a member of the Jiang family with his motorcycle, killing them, and lacked insurance or substantial assets. He borrowed 50K from friends, sold belongings for another 50K, but the court found he still owed 250K.
During the inquiry into Li's assets, the court found that his wife had cancer, and that he was supporting kids. His savings had already been exhausted.
In consideration of Li's efforts to pay, objective hardship, the court actively mediated between the parties, and helped access additional funds for the Jiang family through the traffic accident victim's fund and court aid funds, so that Li only owed an additional 60K to repay- which he did in a few months.
Stop Mechanical Application, Sustainable Outcomes, Subjective Malice
4.  Fujian construction vs. Longyan tech. co (Rental Dispute)  Longyan was ordered to pay 400K owed and return a leased factory.
The parties agreed to an installment plan, but Longyan couldn't perform past the first period, so Plaintiff sought court enforcement, and Longyan was placed on the Judgment Defaulter List.
Longyan explained to the court that their capital scheme had been disrupted, but that they were seeking new investment. Blacklisting would effectively preclude new investors- but a grace period would allow them to recover and satisfy the judgment.
The court accepted this argument, but placed some assets under seal as a guarantee against further default.
New investment emerged and the parties renewed their lease.   Stop Mechanical Application, Sustainable Outcomes
5  Yu v. Property Mgmt Co. (Loan dispute)  Defendant owed Plaintiff about 2 million RMB plus interest. The court mediated a 3 installment payment plan, but Def. defaulted after 2 payments, so the court entered them into the Judgement Defaulter's List.
In consideration of the Defendant's previous performance on the first 2 installments, and the impact that the company's failure would have on its many customers, the court mediated a grace period between the parties allowing the company to operate normally while it completed repayment.
Sustainable Outcomes, Subjective Malice
6  Beijing Construction group vs. Construction Teams (contract dispute)  Defendants owed 2M plus interests and defaulted after two payments under an installment compensation plan. The court found that conditions for entry on the Judgment Defaulter List were met.
During enforcement, however, the court found that the business was viable and that entry onto the list would likely reduce its ability to satisfy the judgment rather than enforce it.
The court encouraged communication between the parties and urged acceptance of a grace period, with the judgment ultimately being satisfied.
Sustainable Outcomes
7.  Anhui Investment Company vs. Wuhu Shopping Cart Co. (Construction Contract)  The defendant owed 9.61 million, so the court froze some of its assets. When meeting with representatives, however, the court learned that this made it very difficult for the company to operate and repay its outstanding debts.
The court recognized that allowing the company to 'save itself' was the best option for all parties and unfroze some assets, to ensure that the businesses and customers were minimally impacted.   Sustainable Outcomes
8.  Chen vs. Construction Company (contract dispute)  The company had paid the majority of money owed to Chen on an arbitration award, but still owed 270,000.  Chen owed his workers wages for the project, and sought enforcement against the Defendant with the court.
During the court's inquiry, it found that the company was still functioning but severely lacking in liquid assets, and the court entered it on to the Judgment Defaulter List.
The company's legal representative explained to the court that it was willing to perform, and that allowing it to continue operations would help it satisfy the judgments. Court agreed, allowing workers to be paid.   Sustainable Outcomes
9.  Li  vs. 58 Employees (Labor Contract Dispute)  Li ran a smaller family clothing production business. He unsuccessfully sought to expand the business, and 58 employees sued him for a total of 560K owed wages. Li sold his house and put 280K towards repaying the salaries.
Court investigation found that Li's business owned some textile machinery that could be enforced against. The machinery however, was not worth much, and more valuable in continuing to let Li earn some income.
The judge coordinated the defendants, who were migrant workers who had returned to their home provinces, creating a wechat group to communicate with them all. The court encouraged settlement for a reduced amount of back wages, and flexible payment; which allowed Li to satisfy the judgment.   Sustainable Outcomes, Subjective Malice, Stop Mechanical Application

title: China Proposes Strengthened Procedures for Formulating State Council Regulations
text:
China’s Ministry of Justice (MOJ) issued on June 5 for a 30-day public comment period a proposed revision ( the  Revision  ) [1]  of the State Council’s Regulations on Procedures for Formulating Administrative Regulations (the  Procedures ). [2]  The Procedures regulate the drafting and modification of nationally-applicable administrative regulations ( xingzheng fagui ) promulgated by the State Council.  They and a similar set of procedures for formulating lower-level rules ( guizhang ), which are issued by State Council departments and by local governments and their departments, and which have also been designated by the State Council for future revision, [3]  were adopted in 2001 and amended in 2017. [4]  Both are legally binding and considered “legislation” under China’s Legislation Law. [5]
Following revisions to the Legislation Law in 2023, and the Chinese Communist Party’s Third Plenum call in July 2024 [6]  to deepen legislative reforms, further improve legislative quality and more thoroughly review the government’s legislative work, the State Council legislative plan for 2025 [7]  scheduled and promptly started the process for revision of the Procedures.   Since the governmental reorganization in 2018, [8]  the MOJ has served the State Council as the coordinator and reviewer, as well as occasional drafter, of State Council administrative regulations and lower-level rules.
The Revision proposes much new language, including the now obligatory affirmation of party leadership, [9]  to be exercised through party committees. [10]   It emphasizes better coordination between legislative priorities and the Party’s and State Council’s policies and plans and reform initiatives generally, as well as with matters of great concern to society, urgently needed in practice and with relatively mature conditions. Adding new provisions on and to implement “scientific, democratic, and law-based legislation,” the Revision strengthens initial assessments as to the necessity, relevance, feasibility and expected results of proposed legislation, as well as its responsiveness to the public’s demands; addresses the formulation of needed supporting provisions; and clarifies how to handle temporary adjustments or suspensions and repeals of administrative regulations.  It also codifies a useful requirement, which was also added to the Legislation Law in 2015, to include a comparison of the original and revised texts, like the one Changhao Wei of NPC Observer prepared for the Revision, [11]  when submitting proposed revisions of administrative regulations to the State Council.
However, most additions elaborate on and tighten specific requirements to better ensure the relevance, quality and efficacy of administrative regulations. The basic procedures for drafting administrative regulations remain the same.  These include important procedural transparency provisions on soliciting public input through such mechanisms as grassroots investigations, symposia, expert appraisal meetings and public hearings in some cases, and require the publication of drafts and explanation thereof to solicit public input for at least 30 days, unless the State Council decides otherwise.  While this rarely happens, the State Council recently promulgated an administrative regulation on government affairs data sharing [12]  without releasing a draft for public comment. Presumably, a public process was deemed unnecessary, since the regulation concerns internal governmental procedures.
The Revision does add a new mechanism to broaden channels for public input, implementing a recent addition to the Legislation Law. The MOJ, referred to in the Procedures as the “legislative affairs institution of the State Council,” is authorized to establish government legislative contact points (outreach offices) to collect views on drafts and its work generally. It will be interesting to see how this new channel will operate.
Unlike the legislative system for laws, draft administrative regulations and rules are typically published for comment once rather than twice, and no statistics on the number of comments received are published, as is done on the National People’s Congress website. [13]  Neither legislative process publishes the actual comments received.
